Aluno
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Ana Claudia Veraldi

Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

B2 (Intermediário) São Paulo, SP Analista de Sistemas — QA 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Ana? -- Introducing Your Work in QA & Tech

Introducing yourself and your work in quality assurance to an international team. Key words: quality assurance, to test, to validate, software, bug, requirement, deadline, remote work, feature, to deploy, scenario, stakeholder. Structures: present perfect for experience (for/since) vs present simple (routine) vs past simple (finished time).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Quality assurance -- the process of testing software to make sure it works before it reaches the user
"I have worked in quality assurance for six years."
To test -- to try software to see if it works and to find problems
"I test every new feature before it goes live."
To validate -- to check that something meets the requirements and works as expected
"We validate the system in expected and unexpected situations."
Software -- the programs and code that run on a computer
"Our team builds software for banks around the world."
Bug -- an error or fault in a program that makes it behave incorrectly
"My job is to catch every bug before the user does."
Requirement -- something the system must do, agreed with the client
"Each test checks one requirement from the client."
Deadline -- the date by which work must be finished
"We have a tight deadline for the release on Friday."
Remote work -- working from home or another place, not the office
"I am looking for full remote work with a company abroad."
Feature -- a specific function or part of a product
"The new payment feature needs more testing."
To deploy -- to release software so that users can use it
"We deploy the new version every two weeks."
Scenario -- a possible situation you test the system in
"I test the login in every scenario, expected and unexpected."
Stakeholder -- a person with an interest in the project: a client, manager or user
"I report bugs clearly so every stakeholder understands the risk."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Quality assurance
To test
To validate
Software
Bug
Requirement
Deadline
Remote work
Feature
To deploy
Scenario
Stakeholder

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana is a systems analyst who works in quality assurance. Every day, she tests and validates new features before her team deploys them. "I have worked in QA for six years," she says. Earlier in her career, she worked at WIPRO, an Indian company, for six years, and she learned a lot about international teams. Right now, she is testing a new payment feature, and the team has just found a critical bug before the deadline. Ana has always used English for technical reading, but now she wants to speak it too, because she is looking for full remote work with a company abroad. Next week, she has a video interview with a stakeholder from another country.

1. Why do we say "I have worked in QA for six years"?
A The experience started in the past and continues up to now (present perfect + for).
B The action is finished and has no link to the present.
C It is a routine she does every single week.
2. "She worked at WIPRO for six years" uses the past simple because:
A The experience continues today.
B It is a finished job, in a finished period of the past.
C It is happening right now.
3. Which sentence about her daily routine is correct?
A "She has tested new features since every day."
B "She tests new features every day."
C "She is testing new features every day for years."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Present Simple vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

How to talk about your career with the right tense.

FormUseExample
Present Perfect
have / has + past participle		Experience and duration up to now, with for / since, when the time is not finished.I have worked here for 6 years.
just + present perfectVery recent news.We have just deployed the new version.
Present Simple
I / you / we + verb (he/she + s)		Facts and routines, what you do every day.I test software every day.
Past Simple
verb + -ed		A finished action, in a finished time (in 2018, at WIPRO, yesterday).I worked at WIPRO for six years.
for vs sincefor + a period (for 6 years) · since + a starting point (since 2018)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I in quality assurance for six years."
"Every day, I new features before release."
"Last year, I a critical bug before the deadline."
"We the new version."
"I have worked in tech 2018."
"I have worked in QA six years."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Listen to the conversation, then put the steps of a job interview in the correct order.

?Mark asks about her work with software and testing.
?They agree to schedule a video interview and follow up by email.
?Ana introduces herself: her name, her role, and her company.
?Ana describes what she is working on right now.
?Ana explains how long she has worked in quality assurance.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Hi, I am Ana, a systems analyst in quality assurance.
I have worked in quality assurance for six years.
I test and validate software before we deploy it.
I am looking for full remote work with an international team.
I find the bug before it reaches the user.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real interview situation.

A recruiter asks: "So, what do you do?" You answer:
A "I do computer things for a company."
B "I am a systems analyst. I test and validate software before we deploy it."
C "I am working in QA since six years."
He asks: "How long have you worked in QA?" The best answer is:
A "I work in QA for six years."
B "I have worked in QA for six years."
C "I am working in QA for six years."
You want to mention a finished job earlier in your career. Which is correct?
A "I worked at WIPRO for six years."
B "I have worked at WIPRO for six years." (still there)
C "I am working at WIPRO for six years."
You did not understand the recruiter. You say:
A "Sorry, could you repeat that, please?"
B "What? I no understand you."
C "Repeat, repeat."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are in a video interview for a remote QA role. The recruiter says: "So, tell me about yourself and your experience." Introduce yourself: your role (present simple), your experience (present perfect, for/since), and the kind of work you are looking for right now. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1Hi, I am Ana, a systems analyst in quality assurance.
2I have worked in QA for six years.
3I test and validate software before we deploy it.
4I am looking for full remote work with an international team.
5Could you repeat that, please?
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

Your Career Story -- Talking About Your Experience

Telling your professional story in a remote interview. Key words: career path, milestone, promotion, background, expertise, challenge, responsibility, to achieve, to specialize, to lead, to mentor, to earn. Structure: present perfect with for/since and ever/never for experience and duration up to now.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Career path -- the whole series of jobs and roles across your working life
"I have built a strong career path in technology."
Milestone -- an important achievement that marks real progress in your career
"Mentoring junior testers is one of my favorite milestones."
Promotion -- a move to a higher, more senior position at work
"I have earned two promotions in six years."
Background -- your past studies, training, and work experience
"My background is in computer science."
Expertise -- deep skill and knowledge in one specific field
"Software testing is my area of expertise."
Challenge -- a demanding task that tests your ability
"Every project brings a new challenge."
Responsibility -- a duty you are trusted to take care of
"Quality is my responsibility on every release."
To achieve -- to reach a goal after real effort
"I have achieved a lot in my career."
To specialize -- to focus your work on one particular area
"I have specialized in finding critical bugs."
To lead -- to guide and take charge of a team or project
"I have led a small QA team since last year."
To mentor -- to guide and support a less experienced colleague
"I have mentored several junior testers."
To earn -- to get something through your work, like a promotion or trust
"I have earned the trust of my stakeholders."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Career path
Milestone
Promotion
Background
Expertise
Challenge
Responsibility
To achieve
To specialize
To lead
To mentor
To earn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana has built a strong career path in technology. She has worked in quality assurance since 2018, and she has been in tech for more than ten years. Her background is in computer science, and her expertise is software testing. She has specialized in finding critical bugs, and every project is a new challenge. She has achieved a lot: she has earned two promotions and has mentored several junior testers — one of her favorite milestones. She has never worked abroad, but she has always wanted to. "Have you ever worked with an international team?" a recruiter asked. "No, I haven't," she said, "but that is my next goal."

1. Why do we say "She has worked in QA since 2018"?
A The experience started in 2018 and continues up to now (present perfect + since).
B The action is finished and has no link to the present.
C It is a plan for the future.
2. "She has been in tech for more than ten years" uses "for" because:
A "for" marks a fixed starting point.
B "for" is used with a period of time (ten years).
C "for" is only used in questions.
3. Which sentence about her experience abroad is correct?
A "She has worked abroad since never."
B "She has never worked abroad."
C "She never work abroad for years."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect with for / since / ever / never

GRAMMAR

How to talk about your experience up to now.

UseFormExample
Experience up to nowhave / has + past participleI have worked in QA.
A period of timefor + periodI have worked here for six years.
A starting pointsince + point in timeI have worked here since 2018.
Questions about experienceever + past participleHave you ever led a team?
No experience up to nowI have never worked abroad. (Do not use the present simple for experience: NOT "I work here since 2018".)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I in quality assurance for six years."
"I have worked in tech 2018."
"I have been in QA six years."
"Have you led a team?"
"I have worked abroad."
"I have two promotions."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Listen to the conversation, then put the steps of the interview in the correct order.

?They agree to continue the interview on Thursday.
?David asks how long she has worked in quality assurance.
?David asks if she has ever worked with an international team.
?Ana explains her experience and her area of expertise.
?David thanks Ana for her application and introduces the role.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Hi, I am Ana. I have worked in QA for six years.
I have been in technology since 2018.
I have specialized in software testing.
Have you ever worked with an international team?
I have never worked abroad, but I have always wanted to.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real interview situation.

The recruiter asks: "How long have you worked in QA?" You answer:
A "I work in QA for six years."
B "I have worked in QA for six years."
C "I am working in QA since six years."
He asks: "Have you ever led a team?" The best answer is:
A "Yes, I have. I have led a small QA team since last year."
B "Yes, I lead a team since last year."
C "Yes, I am leading a team since last year."
You want to say you have no experience working abroad. Which is correct?
A "I have never worked abroad, but I would love to."
B "I have worked abroad since never."
C "I never work abroad for years."
You want to mention a career milestone. Which sounds best?
A "I have earned two promotions and mentored several testers."
B "I earn two promotions since I started."
C "I am earning two promotions for years."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

You are in a video interview for a remote QA role. The recruiter says: "Tell me your career story." Use the present perfect: how long you have worked in QA (for/since), your expertise, a milestone you have achieved, and one thing you have never done but want to. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1I have worked in quality assurance for six years.
2I have been in tech since 2018.
3My expertise is software testing.
4Have you ever worked with a remote team?
5I have never worked abroad, but I have always wanted to.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

A Day in QA -- Describing Your Routine

Describing your typical workday and what you are working on right now, to a remote teammate. Key words: routine, workflow, backlog, to prioritize, to assign, to track, sprint, to collaborate, ongoing, to handle, update, estimate. Structure: present simple (routine) vs present continuous (right now), and stative verbs.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Routine -- a fixed set of tasks you do regularly, in the same way
"My morning routine starts with the team stand-up."
Workflow -- the series of steps a task moves through from start to finish
"Every bug follows the same workflow, from report to fix."
Backlog -- the list of tasks and bugs that are waiting to be done
"I check the backlog first thing every morning."
To prioritize -- to put tasks in order of importance
"I prioritize the critical bugs before the small ones."
To assign -- to give a specific task to a person on the team
"The lead assigns each ticket to one tester."
To track -- to follow the progress of a task over time
"We track every open bug on the same board."
Sprint -- a short, fixed period in which the team finishes planned work
"Our team works in two-week sprints."
To collaborate -- to work together with other people toward the same goal
"I collaborate with developers in three countries."
Ongoing -- happening now and not yet finished
"The migration is an ongoing task this month."
To handle -- to deal with a task, problem, or request
"I handle the urgent bugs while my colleague tests new features."
Update -- a short report on the current progress of your work
"I give a quick update at every stand-up."
Estimate -- a rough calculation of how long a task will take
"I give the manager an estimate for each new test."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Routine
Workflow
Backlog
To prioritize
To assign
To track
Sprint
To collaborate
Ongoing
To handle
Update
Estimate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana works in quality assurance, and every day follows the same routine. Each morning, she checks the backlog and prioritizes the critical bugs. Her team works in two-week sprints, and they track the progress on one board. Right now, Ana is testing a new payment feature, and the developers are fixing two bugs from yesterday. This week, she is collaborating with a team in India. "I know the workflow very well," she says, "so I usually handle the urgent tasks and I give the lead an estimate for each new test." At the moment, she is preparing a short update for the daily stand-up.

1. Why does the text say "she checks the backlog" and not "she is checking the backlog"?
A It is a daily routine, so we use the present simple.
B It is happening only at this moment.
C It is a finished action in the past.
2. "Right now, Ana is testing a new payment feature" uses the present continuous because:
A It is something she does every day.
B It is happening at this moment, in progress.
C It is a fact that is always true.
3. Why does the text say "I know the workflow" and not "I am knowing the workflow"?
A Because it is happening right now.
B "Know" is a stative verb and does not take the -ing form.
C Because it is a finished action.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous

GRAMMAR

How to switch between your routine and what is happening right now.

FormUseExample
Present Simple
I / you / we + verb (he/she + s)		Routines and facts, what you do regularly (every day, usually, always, often).I check the backlog every morning.
Present Continuous
am / is / are + verb-ing		Actions happening now or around now, temporary (right now, at the moment, today, this week).Right now, I am testing the login feature.
He / she / it formAdd -s in the present simple; use is + -ing in the continuous.The lead assigns tickets. She is fixing a bug.
Stative verbsknow, understand, need, want stay simple: "I know the workflow," never "I am knowing".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Every morning, I the backlog."
"Right now, I the payment feature."
"We usually in two-week sprints."
"At the moment, the developers two bugs."
"The lead each ticket to one tester."
"I the workflow very well."

Stage 2: Put the QA Day in Order

Order

Listen to the description, then put the steps of a normal QA day in the correct order.

?The lead assigns each ticket to one tester.
?Ana joins the fifteen-minute morning stand-up and gives her update.
?Ana tests the feature she is handling today and tracks the progress.
?She opens the backlog and prioritizes the critical bugs.
?At the end of the day, the team reviews what they have finished.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Every morning, I check the backlog and prioritize the bugs.
Right now, I am testing the payment feature.
We work in two-week sprints and track everything on one board.
I collaborate with developers in three countries.
At the moment, I am handling two urgent bugs.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

A teammate asks: "What do you do every day?" You answer:
A "I am checking the backlog every morning."
B "Every morning, I check the backlog and prioritize the bugs."
C "I check the backlog right now."
The lead asks: "What are you working on right now?" The best answer is:
A "I test the payment feature every day."
B "Right now, I am testing the payment feature."
C "I am test the payment feature now."
You want to say you understand the process. Which is correct?
A "I know the workflow very well."
B "I am knowing the workflow very well."
C "I am know the workflow very well."
A colleague needs to know your progress. You say:
A "Could you give me an update, please?"
B "Update me now the thing."
C "What you are do now?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new teammate asks you: "Can you walk me through a normal day?" Describe your routine (present simple) and what you are working on this week (present continuous). Include your workflow: the backlog, the sprint, how you track and prioritize the work. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Every morning, I check the backlog and prioritize the bugs.
2Right now, I am testing the payment feature.
3We work in two-week sprints and track everything on one board.
4I collaborate with developers in three countries.
5Could you give me an update, please?
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

Reading a Bug Report -- Listening for Detail

Reading a real bug report and catching every detail in a developer's voice message. Key words: severity, steps to reproduce, expected result, actual result, to crash, root cause, workaround, intermittent, error log, screenshot, environment, to escalate. Structure: past simple vs past continuous to describe what happened.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Severity -- how serious a bug is, from minor to critical
"The severity of this bug is critical."
Steps to reproduce -- the exact actions that make a bug appear again
"Under steps to reproduce, list every action."
Expected result -- what the software is supposed to do
"The expected result is a payment screen."
Actual result -- what the software really does instead
"The actual result is a blank screen."
To crash -- to stop working suddenly and completely
"The page crashed when I clicked Pay now."
Root cause -- the real, underlying reason for a problem
"Lucas is looking for the root cause."
Workaround -- a temporary fix that avoids a problem until it is solved
"We have a workaround: pay from the old page."
Intermittent -- happening only sometimes, not every time
"The bug is not intermittent; it happens every time."
Error log -- the record the system keeps of errors and events
"I attached a copy of the error log."
Screenshot -- a picture of the screen used as evidence
"I added a screenshot to the report."
Environment -- the setup where a bug appears, like browser and version
"The environment was Chrome 120 on staging."
To escalate -- to pass a serious issue to a higher level or team
"We had to escalate the critical bug."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Severity
Steps to reproduce
Expected result
Actual result
To crash
Root cause
Workaround
Intermittent
Error log
Screenshot
Environment
To escalate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Yesterday, Ana was testing the new checkout when she found a bug. She was adding items to the cart, and the total was updating correctly. Then she clicked Pay now, and the page crashed. She wrote a clear report. Under steps to reproduce, she listed every action. The expected result was a payment screen; the actual result was a blank page. She set the severity to critical, attached a screenshot and the error log, and noted the environment: Chrome 120 on staging. The bug was not intermittent; it happened every time. Lucas is still looking for the root cause, but there is a workaround: pay from the old page.

1. Why do we say "She was testing the checkout when she found a bug"?
A The testing was in progress (past continuous) when a short event (found) interrupted it.
B Both actions were long and happened at the same fixed time.
C It describes a habit she has every day.
2. "The page crashed when she clicked Pay now" uses the past simple because:
A both actions were long and in progress.
B both are short, completed events, one right after the other.
C it is a plan for the future.
3. Which sentence about the bug is correct?
A "I was test the checkout when it crashed."
B "I was testing the checkout when it crashed."
C "I testing the checkout when it was crashed."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple vs Past Continuous

GRAMMAR

How to describe what happened when you found a bug.

UseFormExample
Action in progresswas / were + -ingI was testing the checkout.
Sudden, completed eventpast simpleThe page crashed.
Background + interruptionpast continuous + when + past simpleI was testing when it crashed.
Two background actionswhile + past continuousWhile the app was loading, I was watching the log.
Common mistakeDo not drop the -ing: NOT "I was test". Do not use the past continuous for a sudden event: NOT "the page was crashing when I clicked".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I the checkout when it crashed."
"The page when I clicked Pay now."
"While the app , the error appeared."
"The result is a payment screen."
"The result is a blank screen."
"The bug is not ; it happens every time."

Stage 2: Put the Bug Story in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of the bug in the correct order.

?The whole page crashed and showed a blank screen.
?She set the severity to critical and sent the report to the developer.
?Ana was testing the new checkout page during the sprint.
?Ana wrote a clear bug report with the steps to reproduce.
?She added three items to the cart and clicked Pay now.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I was testing the checkout when the page crashed.
The expected result is a payment screen.
The actual result is a blank screen.
The severity is critical because no one can pay.
The bug is not intermittent; it happens every time.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real QA situation.

A developer asks: "What were you doing when the app crashed?" You answer:
A "I was testing the checkout when it crashed."
B "I test the checkout when it crashes."
C "I am testing the checkout when it crashed."
You want to describe the problem clearly. Which is correct?
A "The expected result is a payment screen, but the actual result is a blank page."
B "The expect result is a payment screen."
C "The result actual is a blank page."
The developer asks: "Is it intermittent?" The best answer is:
A "No, it is not intermittent; it happens every time."
B "Yes, it is intermittent; it happens every time."
C "No, it is intermittent only sometimes."
You want to give the severity. Which sounds best?
A "The severity is critical, because no customer can pay."
B "The severity is critical when I clicked Pay now."
C "It has a severity for six years."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

You are in a stand-up with your remote team, and a developer asks about the bug you found yesterday. Report it: what you were doing when it happened, the steps to reproduce, the expected and actual result, and the severity. Use the past simple and past continuous. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1I was testing the checkout when the page crashed.
2The expected result is a payment screen; the actual result is a blank page.
3The severity is critical, because no one can pay.
4The bug is not intermittent; it happens every time.
5I attached a screenshot and the error log.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

The Daily Stand-Up -- Giving Updates

Giving a clear daily stand-up update to your team: what you did yesterday, what you have done so far, and your blockers. Key words: stand-up, blocker, recap, to wrap up, to sync up, on track, behind schedule, heads-up, to follow up, deliverable, to loop in, action item. Structure: present perfect (news now) vs past simple (a finished time).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Stand-up -- a short daily meeting where the team shares quick progress updates
"We start every morning with a fifteen-minute stand-up."
Blocker -- something that stops you from making progress on a task
"My only blocker is that I am waiting for the new build."
Recap -- a short summary of what has happened so far
"Here is a quick recap of yesterday's testing."
To wrap up -- to finish or complete a task or discussion
"Let us wrap up the release decision this week."
To sync up -- to meet briefly to share information and stay aligned
"Can we sync up about the release this afternoon?"
On track -- going as planned, on schedule
"I have not finished yet, but I am on track."
Behind schedule -- later than the planned time
"I am a little behind schedule on the payment tests."
Heads-up -- an early warning so someone can get ready
"Just a heads-up: the test server will be down at noon."
To follow up -- to check on something or continue it after a first contact
"I will follow up with the dev team after the stand-up."
Deliverable -- a specific piece of work you must finish and hand over
"The test report is my main deliverable for this sprint."
To loop in -- to add someone to a message so they have the information
"Can you loop in the developers on this thread?"
Action item -- a specific task the team agrees to do after a meeting
"We have one action item: sync up about the release date."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Stand-up
Blocker
Recap
To wrap up
To sync up
On track
Behind schedule
Heads-up
To follow up
Deliverable
To loop in
Action item

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is nine in the morning, and Ana is ready for the daily stand-up. She gives a quick recap. Yesterday, she tested the checkout page and reported two bugs. So far this morning, she has found one more bug, and she has already written the first report. Today, she is running the payment tests, and she is on track. She has one blocker: she is waiting for the new build, so she is a little behind schedule. Marco says he will follow up with the developers and loop her in. They agree on one action item: they will sync up about the release this afternoon and wrap up the decision.

1. Why does the text say "Yesterday, she tested the checkout page" and not "she has tested"?
A "Yesterday" is a finished past time, so we use the past simple.
B It is happening at this moment.
C It is a general fact that is always true.
2. "So far this morning, she has found one more bug" uses the present perfect because:
A It happened at a specific, finished time.
B It is the news up to now, with no specific time.
C It is a plan for tomorrow.
3. In the report, "she has already written the first report" tells us that:
A She will write it later today.
B The report is finished now, and that is the news.
C She wrote it a long time ago and it does not matter now.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Past Simple

GRAMMAR

How to switch between a finished time and the news right now.

FormUseExample
Past Simple
verb + -ed / irregular		A finished action at a specific past time (yesterday, on Monday, last week, at 9 am).Yesterday, I tested the checkout page.
Present Perfect
have / has + past participle		News and results that matter now, with no specific time (just, already, yet, so far).So far, I have found two bugs.
Questions & negativesPresent perfect for news: have/has + participle. Past simple for a set time: did + base verb.Have you written it yet? / Did you write it yesterday?
Watch outNever use a specific past time with the present perfect, and learn your irregular participles: run (not ran), written (not wrote), done (not did).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Yesterday, I the checkout page."
"So far, I three bugs."
"I the login bug on Monday."
"The team has just the sprint."
"I the payment tests yet."
"The team has all the tests."

Stage 2: Put the Stand-Up in Order

Order

Listen to the description, then put the steps of a daily stand-up in the correct order.

?She says what she is working on today and shares one blocker.
?At nine o'clock, the whole team meets for the fifteen-minute stand-up.
?Marco turns the blocker into an action item and offers to follow up with the dev team.
?Ana gives a quick recap of what she did yesterday.
?After the stand-up, the team syncs up on the release details.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Yesterday, I tested the checkout page and reported two bugs.
So far, I have found three bugs and fixed one.
Today, I am running the payment tests, and I am on track.
I have one blocker: I am waiting for the new build.
Can we sync up about the release this afternoon?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

The lead asks: "What did you do yesterday?" You answer:
A "I have tested the checkout yesterday."
B "Yesterday, I tested the checkout and reported two bugs."
C "I test the checkout every day."
The lead asks: "What have you done so far?" The best answer is:
A "So far, I have found three bugs and written one report."
B "So far, I found three bugs yesterday."
C "So far, I will find three bugs."
You want to report a blocker in the stand-up. You say:
A "I have one blocker: I am waiting for the new build."
B "I am blocker the build now."
C "Yesterday I have a blocker now."
You want to schedule a quick meeting later. You say:
A "Can we sync up about the release this afternoon?"
B "We sync up the release now yesterday."
C "I have sync up the release already."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Your team lead asks you: "Can you give me your stand-up update?" Tell him what you did yesterday (past simple), what you have done so far this morning (present perfect), what you are working on today, and one blocker with an action item. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Yesterday, I tested the checkout page and reported two bugs.
2So far, I have found three bugs and fixed one.
3Today, I am running the payment tests, and I am on track.
4I have one blocker: I am waiting for the new build.
5Can we sync up about the release this afternoon?
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Understanding Different Accents -- Indian English

Reading about a call with a colleague in Bangalore and training listening strategies and clarifying questions. Key words: accent, intonation, word stress, pace, to clarify, to rephrase, to catch, filler word, to mishear, context, to confirm, unclear. Structure: clarifying questions to check understanding.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Accent -- the way people from a country or region pronounce a language
"Rahul speaks English with an Indian accent."
Intonation -- the way the voice rises and falls when we speak
"Her intonation goes up at the end of a question."
Word stress -- the part of a word we say with more force
"In deploy, the word stress is on the second part."
Pace -- the speed at which someone speaks
"He spoke at a fast pace, so I asked him to slow down."
To clarify -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify what you mean by staging?"
To rephrase -- to say the same idea again using different words
"I did not understand, so she rephrased the question."
To catch -- to hear and understand what someone says
"Sorry, I did not catch the last word."
Filler word -- a small sound or word, like um, used while thinking
"He used a few filler words while he found the answer."
To mishear -- to hear something wrongly
"I misheard the time; I thought he said nine, not five."
Context -- the situation around a word that helps you guess its meaning
"Use the context to guess a word you do not know."
To confirm -- to check that something is true or correct
"Let me confirm the details before we finish."
Unclear -- hard to hear or understand
"The line was unclear, so I asked him to repeat it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Accent
Intonation
Word stress
Pace
To clarify
To rephrase
To catch
Filler word
To mishear
Context
To confirm
Unclear

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

On a call with Rahul, Ana could not always catch every word. His accent, his intonation, and his pace were new to her. But she used good strategies. When the line was unclear, she used the context to guess. When she misheard a detail, she asked a clarifying question: "Could you say that again?" When she wanted to check a fact, she said, "So you are saying the test failed twice?" to confirm it. Asking Rahul to rephrase was never a problem.

1. Why do we say "Could you say that again?" instead of "Say that again"?
A "Could you...?" is a polite request; "Say that again" sounds like an order.
B They mean different things; one is about the past.
C "Could you...?" is only for questions about the future.
2. "So you are saying the test failed twice?" is used to:
A ask about the meaning of a single word.
B confirm what you heard.
C change the subject of the call.
3. Which question asks about the meaning of ONE word?
A "Could you spell that name for me?"
B "So you are saying it is not ready?"
C "What do you mean by staging?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Clarifying Questions

GRAMMAR

How to check what you heard, politely and clearly.

FunctionFormExample
Ask to repeat / slow downCould you + base verb ...?Could you repeat that?
Ask about one word or ideaWhat do you mean by + noun?What do you mean by staging?
Confirm what you heardSo you are saying (that) + clause?So you are saying it failed twice?
Check a detailCould you spell / say ...?Could you spell the name for me?
Common mistakeDo not say "What means staging?"; say "What do you mean by staging?". Start a request with "Could you...?", not just "Repeat, please."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Could you what you mean by staging?"
"Sorry, I did not the last word."
"Could you the question, please?"
"Let me the details before we finish."
"Use the to guess a difficult word."
"Rahul speaks English with an Indian ."

Stage 2: Put the Call in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's call in the correct order.

?When she misheard a detail, she used the context to guess it.
?She confirmed the important points before the call ended.
?Ana joined the video call with Rahul from the Bangalore team.
?She asked a clarifying question and asked Rahul to slow down.
?She listened for the main idea and did not stop at every word.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?
What do you mean by staging?
So you are saying the test failed twice, right?
Could you spell that name for me, please?
I did not catch the last part. Could you repeat it?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real call situation.

A colleague speaks fast and you missed a word. You say:
A "Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?"
B "Repeat."
C "You speak very bad."
You do not know the word "staging" in the sentence. You ask:
A "What means staging?"
B "What do you mean by staging?"
C "Staging is what?"
You want to confirm a fact you heard: the deadline is Friday. You say:
A "So you are saying the deadline is Friday, right?"
B "The deadline is Friday when I clicked."
C "Are you the deadline Friday?"
The line is unclear and you missed a name. The best move is:
A "Could you spell that name for me, please?"
B Say nothing and hope you guessed right.
C "Your name is unclear for me."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

You are on a video call with a colleague who has a new accent and a fast pace. He gives you a quick update, but the line is unclear. Show how you would handle it: ask him to repeat or slow down, ask about one word you do not know, and confirm the key facts before the end. Use clarifying questions. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1Sorry, could you say that again, a little slower?
2What do you mean by staging?
3So you are saying the test failed twice, right?
4Could you spell that name for me, please?
5I did not catch the last part. Could you repeat it?
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

Explaining a Test Scenario -- Sequencing

Walking a new teammate through a test case step by step: the precondition, the input, what you do to trigger the check, the outcome, and one edge case. Key words: test case, precondition, input, outcome, edge case, sequence, to walk through, to set up, to trigger, to proceed, to submit, straightforward. Structure: sequencers and time linkers (first, then, next, after that, finally).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Test case -- a written set of steps and the expected result for one function
"Let me walk you through the test case for the login page."
Precondition -- something that must be ready or true before the test starts
"The precondition is that the test account already exists."
Input -- the data or information you enter into the system
"The input is the email and the password."
Outcome -- the final result you get after the steps
"The expected outcome is that the dashboard opens."
Edge case -- an unusual situation at the limit of normal use
"As an edge case, I try a wrong password."
Sequence -- the fixed order in which the steps happen
"Follow the sequence and the test is easy."
To walk through -- to explain something step by step, slowly and clearly
"Can you walk me through this test scenario?"
To set up -- to prepare something before you use it
"First, I set up the test account."
To trigger -- to make an action or event happen
"I click the button to trigger the check."
To proceed -- to continue to the next step
"Once the page loads, you can proceed."
To submit -- to send information or a form to the system
"Finally, I submit the report."
Straightforward -- simple, clear, and easy to follow
"The whole sequence is straightforward."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Test case
Precondition
Input
Outcome
Edge case
Sequence
To walk through
To set up
To trigger
To proceed
To submit
Straightforward

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana is helping Lucas, a new teammate. She walks him through a test case for the login page. First, she checks the precondition: the test account must already exist. Then she opens the login page and enters the input, the email and the password. Next, she clicks the button to trigger the check. After that, she waits for the outcome: the user reaches the dashboard. Before she writes the report, she also tries one edge case, a wrong password. Finally, she saves the results and submits the report. The whole sequence is straightforward once you follow the steps in order.

1. Which word introduces the very FIRST step in the sequence?
A "First"
B "Finally"
C "After that"
2. "After that, she waits for the outcome." What does "after that" tell us?
A This is the first step.
B This step comes right after the previous one.
C This step happens at the same time as the input.
3. Which word marks the LAST step in the sequence?
A "Next"
B "Before"
C "Finally"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Sequencers and Time Linkers

GRAMMAR

How to put the steps of a process in a clear order.

SequencerUseExample
First, / To start,The very first step of the process.First, I set up the test account.
Then / Next, / After that,The following steps, one after another in order.Then I enter the input. After that, I click the button.
Before / OnceOne action in relation to another (which happens first).Before you proceed, check the precondition. Once it loads, read the outcome.
Finally,The last step. Use a comma after First, Next, After that and Finally at the start of a sentence; "then" usually takes no comma.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

", I set up the test account."
"Then I enter the , the email and the password."
"Next, I click the button to the check."
"After that, I wait for the ."
"Before I finish, I try one case."
", I save the results and submit the report."

Stage 2: Put the Test Scenario in Order

Order

Listen to the walk-through, then put the steps of the test case in the correct order.

?Next, click the button to trigger the login check.
?First, set up the test account and open the login page.
?Finally, save the results and submit the report.
?Then enter the input: the email and the password.
?After that, wait for the outcome: the dashboard opens.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

First, I set up the test account and open the login page.
Then I enter the input and click the button to trigger the check.
After that, I wait for the outcome on the screen.
Before I finish, I also try one edge case with a wrong password.
Finally, I save the results and submit the report.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

A new teammate asks you to explain the test. You start with:
A "First, I set up the test account and open the login page."
B "Finally the test account I set up."
C "I set up finally the account first then."
You want to describe the next two steps in order. You say:
A "Finally I enter the input, then first I click the button."
B "Then I enter the input. After that, I click the button to trigger the check."
C "After that first I trigger then the input."
You reach the last step of the walk-through. You say:
A "Finally, I save the results and submit the report."
B "First, I save the results and submit the report."
C "Before, I save the results and submit the report."
You want to say one action must happen before another. You say:
A "Before you proceed, check the precondition."
B "You proceed before check the precondition after."
C "Finally proceed before the precondition."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new teammate asks you: "Can you walk me through one of your test cases?" Explain it step by step. Use First, Then, Next, After that, and Finally. Include the precondition, the input, what you do to trigger the check, the outcome, and one edge case. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Let me walk you through this test case step by step.
2First, set up the account, then enter the input.
3Next, click the button to trigger the check.
4After that, wait for the outcome on the screen.
5Finally, save the results and submit the report.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Checkpoint -- Review of Block 1

A review meeting with Ana's manager that consolidates Block 1 (weeks 1-7). Review words: requirement, to deploy, milestone, challenge, workflow, to prioritize, severity, root cause, blocker, to follow up, to clarify, edge case. Structure: review of the Block 1 tenses (present perfect, past simple, present simple, present continuous).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Review the key words from Block 1. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Requirement -- something the software must do
"When a requirement is not clear, I clarify it before I start."
To deploy -- to put new code on a server so people can use it
"In week five, the team deployed a difficult feature on time."
Milestone -- an important point or achievement in a project
"My first milestone was testing a hard feature in week five."
Challenge -- a difficult task that tests your skills
"My biggest challenge was a blocker in week three."
Workflow -- the steps a task goes through from start to finish
"My workflow starts with the backlog every morning."
To prioritize -- to decide what is most important to do first
"I have learned to prioritize the bugs by severity."
Severity -- how serious a bug is
"I sort the bugs by severity, from critical to minor."
Root cause -- the real reason a problem happens
"When a test fails, I look for the root cause first."
Blocker -- something that stops you from making progress
"A broken environment was a blocker for a whole day."
To follow up -- to check on something again later
"I raised the blocker and followed up until it was fixed."
To clarify -- to make something clearer or easier to understand
"Could you clarify what you mean by this requirement?"
Edge case -- an unusual situation at the limit of what is expected
"Marcus told me to think about the edge cases early."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Requirement
To deploy
Milestone
Challenge
Workflow
To prioritize
Severity
Root cause
Blocker
To follow up
To clarify
Edge case

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In her review, Ana used three tenses without thinking about them. She said, "I have tested many features this block" — the present perfect, for her experience up to now. She said, "In week five, I tested a difficult feature" — the past simple, for a finished moment. And she said, "Every morning I check the backlog" — the present simple, for her routine. When Marcus asked what she was doing at that moment, she answered, "Right now I am working on an edge case" — the present continuous.

1. Why does Ana say "I have tested many features this block" and not "I tested"?
A The block is still going, so she uses the present perfect for experience up to now.
B Both are wrong; she should use the present continuous.
C "Have tested" is only for the future.
2. "In week five, I tested a difficult feature" uses the past simple because:
A it is a daily routine.
B it is a finished action at a specific past time.
C it is happening right now.
3. Which sentence describes something in progress at the moment of speaking?
A "Every morning I check the backlog."
B "In week five, I tested a feature."
C "Right now I am working on an edge case."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Block 1 Tenses

GRAMMAR

One review meeting, four tenses. Choose the tense by the kind of time.

TenseUseExample
Present perfectExperience up to now (no exact time)I have tested many features this block.
Past simpleA finished action at a specific past timeIn week five, I tested a hard feature.
Present simpleA routine or habitEvery morning I check the backlog.
Present continuousIn progress right nowRight now I am working on an edge case.
Common mistakeDo not say "I have tested it yesterday"; with a past time, use the past simple: "I tested it yesterday."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I have learned to the bugs by severity."
"When a test fails, I look for the ."
"My biggest was a blocker in week three."
"I raised it in the stand-up and until it was fixed."
"When a requirement is not clear, I it before I start."
"In week five, the team a difficult feature."

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's review in the correct order.

?She explained her workflow and how she prioritizes bugs by severity.
?Marcus advised her to clarify unclear requirements and think about edge cases.
?Ana prepared a short summary before her review meeting with Marcus.
?She described her biggest challenge, a blocker in week three, and how she followed up.
?She talked about her milestone in week five, when she helped deploy a feature.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I have tested many features this block.
In week five, I tested a difficult feature.
Every morning I check the backlog and prioritize by severity.
My biggest challenge was a blocker, but I followed up.
When a requirement is not clear, I clarify it first.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each review-meeting situation.

Your manager asks what you have achieved this block. You say:
A "I have tested three new features and learned to write clearer bug reports."
B "I test three features tomorrow."
C "I am achievement good this block."
You want to describe a finished event from week five. You say:
A "In week five, I have tested a feature."
B "In week five, I tested a difficult feature."
C "In week five, I am testing a feature."
A requirement is not clear before you start. The best move is:
A "Could you clarify what you mean by this requirement?"
B Start the work and hope you guessed right.
C "The requirement is unclear for me, bye."
A bug is very serious and stops many users. You describe its:
A workflow.
B high severity.
C milestone.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

It is your own review meeting. In one minute, tell your manager about your first block of English: a milestone you are proud of, one challenge you had, and what you do in your daily workflow. Use the present perfect for your experience, the past simple for a finished event, and the present simple for your routine. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1I have tested many features this block.
2In week five, I tested a difficult feature.
3Every morning I check the backlog and prioritize by severity.
4My biggest challenge was a blocker, but I followed up.
5When a requirement is not clear, I clarify it first.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Talking About Requirements -- Asking & Confirming

Joining a requirements call with a product owner to clarify a new feature before you test it: the use case, the end user, the scope, the constraints and the acceptance criteria. Key words: specification, scope, acceptance criteria, use case, assumption, constraint, end user, to gather, to sign off, to double-check, ambiguous, feasible. Structure: question forms and indirect questions (Could you tell me what the client wants?).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Specification -- a detailed written description of what the software must do
"The specification says the report must be a PDF."
Scope -- the boundaries of what a project includes and excludes
"Do you know if this feature is in scope?"
Acceptance criteria -- the conditions a feature must meet to be accepted
"The acceptance criteria are a PDF, under ten megabytes."
Use case -- how a user interacts with the system to reach a goal
"Could you tell me what the main use case is?"
Assumption -- something you treat as true without checking it
"Never make an assumption; ask instead."
Constraint -- a limit or restriction you have to work within
"The file size is a hard constraint."
End user -- the real person who will actually use the software
"Who is the end user of this feature?"
To gather -- to collect information from different people or sources
"A good analyst gathers the requirements by asking."
To sign off -- to formally approve that something is correct or complete
"The client will sign off on the final requirements."
To double-check -- to check something again to be completely sure
"Let me double-check the requirements with you."
Ambiguous -- not clear because it has more than one possible meaning
"The ticket was a little ambiguous, so I asked."
Feasible -- possible to do within the time and the limits you have
"The constraints decide what is feasible."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Specification
Scope
Acceptance criteria
Use case
Assumption
Constraint
End user
To gather
To sign off
To double-check
Ambiguous
Feasible

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana is on a requirements call with Bruno, the product owner. She never makes an assumption; she asks. First she reads the specification, but it is a little ambiguous, so she asks questions. "Could you tell me what the main use case is?" she asks. Bruno explains that the end user clicks Export and gets a PDF. "Do you know if the report has to include the charts?" Ana asks. Then she checks the scope and the constraints: "Can I ask what the constraint on the file size is?" Before the call ends, she repeats everything back to confirm her understanding and to double-check the acceptance criteria. Notice how her polite questions use statement word order: "what the use case is", not "what is the use case".

1. Which is the correct indirect question?
A "Could you tell me what does the client want?"
B "Could you tell me what the client wants?"
C "Could you tell me what want the client?"
2. For a yes/no idea, which indirect question is correct?
A "Do you know is the feature in scope?"
B "Do you know if the feature is in scope?"
C "Do you know the feature is in scope if?"
3. In an indirect question, the word order after the wh-word is...
A the same as a statement: subject before verb, no do/does.
B always inverted, like a direct question.
C with an extra "do" or "does" added.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Question Forms & Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

How to ask polite, clear questions to gather requirements.

TypeDirect questionIndirect (polite) question
Wh- questionWhat does the client want?Could you tell me what the client wants?
Yes/No questionIs the feature in scope?Do you know if the feature is in scope?
Word orderAfter the wh-word or "if/whether", use statement order (subject + verb). Drop do/does/did: "where the file is", not "where is the file".
OpenersCould you tell me... · Do you know... · Can I ask... · I was wondering whether...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"Before I start, I always read the for the feature."
"Could you tell me what the main is?"
"Do you know if this feature is in ?"
"The ticket is , so I need to clarify it."
"Just to , the file must be a PDF."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana clarifies a requirement, then put the steps in the correct order.

?Next, confirm the scope: what is included and what is not.
?First, read the specification for the new feature.
?Finally, repeat everything back to confirm you understood.
?Then ask what the main use case is and who the end user is.
?After that, check the constraints, like the deadline and the file size.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Could you tell me what the main use case is?
Do you know if this feature is in scope?
Can I ask what the constraint on the file size is?
Just to confirm, the acceptance criteria are a PDF under ten megabytes.
Let me double-check the requirements before I write the test cases.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

A requirement is not clear. You want to ask about it politely. You say:
A "Could you tell me what the end user needs here?"
B "Tell me what needs the end user here now."
C "What the end user needs, you tell me?"
You want to check a yes/no point about the feature. You ask:
A "Do you know is this in the scope of the sprint?"
B "Do you know if this is in the scope of the sprint?"
C "Do you know this is in scope if the sprint?"
You want to repeat the requirement back to make sure. You say:
A "Just to confirm, the report must be a PDF under ten megabytes, right?"
B "I assume the report is fine, so I will not ask."
C "Confirm just the report PDF ten megabytes to."
The client gives you a limit on the delivery date. In English, this is a:
A use case
B constraint
C end user

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a product owner gives you a new feature to test, but the ticket is ambiguous. Ask three questions to clarify the requirements: one about the use case, one about the scope, and one about a constraint. Use indirect questions ("Could you tell me...", "Do you know if..."). Then confirm the acceptance criteria back in one sentence. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Could you tell me what the main use case is?
2Do you know if this feature is in scope?
3Can I ask what the constraint on the file size is?
4Just to confirm, the acceptance criteria are a PDF under ten megabytes.
5Let me double-check the requirements before I write the test cases.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

Reporting a Problem -- Being Clear & Precise

Ana reports a bug to stakeholders after it is found in production: the impact, the symptom, the frequency, and what was done. New words: impact, symptom, frequency, to resolve, regression, reproducible, patch, downtime, to roll back, evidence, concise, vague. Grammar: the passive voice for reporting problems (the bug was found; a patch was deployed; it has been resolved).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the words for reporting a problem clearly. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Impact -- how badly a problem affects users or the business
"The impact was high: fifteen percent of users could not pay."
Symptom -- a visible sign that something is wrong
"The symptom was a blank screen after the user clicked Pay."
Frequency -- how often a problem happens
"The bug happened with high frequency during peak hours."
To resolve -- to fix a problem and close it
"The problem has been resolved and a new test was added."
Regression -- when something that worked before breaks after a change
"The bug was a regression from the last sprint."
Reproducible -- able to be made to happen again in the same way
"The bug was reproducible, so I added the exact steps."
Patch -- a small update that fixes a specific problem
"A patch was deployed within the hour."
Downtime -- a period when the system is not available to users
"Downtime was kept under twenty minutes."
To roll back -- to return the software to an earlier working version
"The broken release was rolled back immediately."
Evidence -- facts, like logs or screenshots, that show what happened
"I attached a screenshot and the logs as evidence."
Concise -- giving clear information in few words
"A good report is concise: five short lines, no story."
Vague -- not clear or not specific enough
"Do not be vague; say exactly how many users were affected."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Impact
Symptom
Frequency
To resolve
Regression
Reproducible
Patch
Downtime
To roll back
Evidence
Concise
Vague

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When Ana wrote her incident report, she used the passive voice again and again. She wrote, "A serious bug was found in production" — the event, without naming who found it. She wrote, "The issue was escalated to the backend team and a patch was deployed" — the actions, because the names did not matter, only what was done. And she closed with, "The problem has been resolved" — a finished action that is still true now. The passive keeps a report neutral, clean and precise.

1. Why does Ana write "A bug was found" instead of "Someone found a bug"?
A The event matters, not who did it, so she uses the passive voice.
B "Was found" is only used for the future.
C The two sentences mean different things.
2. "A patch was deployed" is formed with:
A a past participle only, with no verb 'be'.
B a form of 'be' (was) plus a past participle (deployed).
C the present continuous.
3. Which sentence reports a finished action that is still true now?
A "A bug was found in production."
B "The issue was escalated to the team."
C "The problem has been resolved."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Passive Voice

GRAMMAR

Passive = a form of be + the past participle. Use it in a report when the action or the result matters more than who did it.

FormUseExample
was / were + past participleReport a past action; the name is not importantA patch was deployed.
is / are + past participleA current fact or a ruleThe bug is reproduced in three steps.
has / have been + past participleA finished action, still relevant nowThe problem has been resolved.
Common mistakeDo not drop the 'be' or the participle: not "A patch deployed" and not "It was resolve" — say "A patch was deployed" and "It was resolved."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"A serious bug in production this morning."
"The issue to the backend team."
"A was deployed within the hour."
"The problem has been and a new test was added."
"The bug is , so the steps are attached."
"Lead with the : how many users are affected."

Stage 2: Put the Report in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's report in the correct order.

?She reported what was done in the passive voice: the issue was escalated and a patch was deployed.
?She confirmed that the problem had been resolved and a new test was added.
?She opened with the impact: about fifteen percent of users could not pay.
?She explained the root cause: a regression from the last sprint.
?She described the symptom: a blank screen after the user clicked Pay.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

A bug was found in production this morning.
The impact was high, with about fifteen percent of users affected.
The issue was escalated and a patch was deployed.
It was a regression, and it has been resolved.
The steps are reproducible, and a screenshot is attached.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each reporting situation.

You open your report to stakeholders. The best first line is:
A "The impact was high: about fifteen percent of users could not pay."
B "Let me tell you the whole story of my morning."
C "Some problems were seen somewhere."
You want to report an action without naming people. You say:
A "John and Maria escalated the issue and deployed a patch."
B "The issue was escalated and a patch was deployed."
C "The issue is escalate and a patch deploy."
A stakeholder asks if it is fixed for good. You answer:
A "It has been resolved, and a new test was added so it cannot return."
B "Maybe, I did not really check."
C "It resolve yesterday by someone."
A bug happens only sometimes, at busy times. To describe this, you talk about its:
A patch.
B frequency.
C downtime.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A bug appears in a feature you tested. In one minute, give a clear incident report to your stakeholders: start with the impact, then the symptom and how often it happens, then say what was done using the passive voice (was escalated, was deployed), and finish by saying it has been resolved. Keep it short and precise.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1A bug was found in production this morning.
2The impact was high, with about fifteen percent of users affected.
3The issue was escalated and a patch was deployed.
4It was a regression, and it has been resolved.
5The steps are reproducible, and a screenshot is attached.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

If This, Then That -- First Conditional

Planning a Friday rollout with a developer and doing a quick risk analysis: talking about cause and effect in testing -- what will happen if a condition is true. Key words: condition, dependency, threshold, load, to overload, bottleneck, timeout, fallback, retry, rollout, to cascade, consequence. Structure: first conditional for real situations (If the load is high, the app will slow down).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Condition -- a state that must be true for something else to happen
"If this condition is true, the test will fail."
Dependency -- something a task or feature relies on to work
"If a dependency is down, the feature will not load."
Threshold -- the level or point at which something starts to happen
"If the load passes the threshold, the server slows down."
Load -- the amount of traffic or work a system has to handle
"If the load is high, the app will respond slowly."
To overload -- to give a system more work than it can handle
"If too many users log in, they will overload the server."
Bottleneck -- a single slow point that holds up the whole system
"If the database is a bottleneck, every page will be slow."
Timeout -- an error when a system waits too long for a response
"If the server is slow, the request will end in a timeout."
Fallback -- a backup option the system uses if the main one fails
"If the main service fails, the fallback will take over."
Retry -- an automatic new attempt after something fails
"If the first attempt fails, the retry will run automatically."
Rollout -- the process of releasing a feature to users
"If the tests pass, the rollout will happen on Friday."
To cascade -- to spread from one failure to many in a chain
"If one service fails, the errors will cascade."
Consequence -- the result or effect that follows an action
"If we skip the test, the consequence will be a bug in production."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Condition
Dependency
Threshold
Load
To overload
Bottleneck
Timeout
Fallback
Retry
Rollout
To cascade
Consequence

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before a rollout, Ana thinks in cause and effect. She looks at each condition and asks what will happen next. If the load is high on Monday morning, the app will slow down. If too many users arrive at once, they will overload the server. And if the database is a bottleneck, every page will be slow. She also checks the dependencies: if one service fails, the errors will cascade through the system. So the team adds a fallback. If the main service ends in a timeout, the app will switch to the backup. Notice the pattern: the condition uses the present simple ("if the load is high"), and the consequence uses "will" ("the app will slow down"). Never "will" after "if".

1. Which is the correct first conditional sentence?
A "If the load will be high, the app slows down."
B "If the load is high, the app will slow down."
C "If the load is high, the app slow down."
2. In the first conditional, the verb after "if" is in the...
A present simple, with no "will".
B future, always with "will".
C past simple.
3. We use the first conditional to talk about...
A a real cause and its likely effect.
B an impossible, imaginary situation.
C something that finished in the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional (Real Situations)

GRAMMAR

How to talk about a real cause and its likely effect in testing.

PartFormExample
If-clause (cause)If + present simpleIf the load is high,
Result (effect)will + base verbthe app will slow down.
Negativewill not / won'tIf we add a fallback, the users won't see an error.
Questionwill + subject...?If the load is high, will the app crash?
Key rulePresent simple after if (never "will" after if). You can swap the order: "The app will slow down if the load is high" (no comma when the if-clause is second).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"If the traffic passes the , the service will slow down."
"If the main service fails, the will take over."
"If one service goes down, the errors through the system."
"If too many users log in at once, they will the server."
"If we skip this test, the will be a bug in production."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana does a quick risk analysis, then put the steps in the correct order.

?After that, run a load test to find the threshold.
?First, list the main features and their dependencies.
?Finally, report the conditions to the team before the rollout.
?Then, for each one, ask what will happen if it fails.
?Next, add a fallback for the highest-risk part.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If the load is high, the app will slow down.
If the main service times out, the fallback will take over.
If we go over the threshold, the requests will time out.
If one service fails, the errors will cascade through the system.
If we run the load test first, we will find the bottleneck.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

You want to warn the team about a likely effect of high traffic. You say:
A "If the load is high on Monday, the app will slow down."
B "If the load will be high on Monday, the app slows down."
C "If the load high on Monday, the app will slowing down."
A developer asks what happens when the main service stops responding. You explain:
A "If the service will time out, the app crashed."
B "If the service times out, the app will switch to the fallback."
C "If the service times out, the app switching to the fallback."
You want to say a slow database will hold up the whole system. In English, the slow part is a:
A threshold
B bottleneck
C rollout
You want to convince the team to test before releasing. You argue:
A "If we skip the load test, the errors will cascade in production."
B "If we will skip the load test, the errors cascade in production."
C "We skip the load test if the errors cascade in production."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your team is planning a rollout for Friday. Describe three risks using the first conditional: one about the load, one about a dependency or bottleneck, and one about skipping a test. For each one, say the cause and the effect ("If..., the app will..."). Then say what you will do to prevent it. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1If the load is high, the app will slow down.
2If the main service times out, the fallback will take over.
3If one service fails, the errors will cascade.
4If we skip the test, the consequence will be a bug in production.
5If we run the load test first, we will find the bottleneck.
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

What Would You Do? -- Second Conditional

Ana has limited time before a release and must decide what to test. She weighs trade-offs and plans for "what if". New words: trade-off, hypothetical, contingency, to mitigate, downside, to postpone, to justify, to assess, alternative, reasonable, to reconsider, cost-effective. Grammar: the second conditional for hypothetical situations (If I had more time, I would automate the checkout tests).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the words for making smart decisions under pressure. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Trade-off -- a balance between two good things you cannot both fully have
"It is a trade-off between speed and safety."
Hypothetical -- imagined, not real; used to think about a possible situation
"She asked herself a hypothetical question: what if I had more time?"
Contingency -- a plan for something that might go wrong
"Her contingency plan was to roll back to the old version."
To mitigate -- to make a risk or a problem less serious
"To mitigate the risk, she kept the old version ready."
Downside -- the negative part or disadvantage of a choice
"The downside was that a small visual bug might slip through."
To postpone -- to move something to a later time
"She decided to postpone the low-risk tests to the next sprint."
To justify -- to give a good reason for a decision
"She could justify that choice to her team in one line."
To assess -- to judge how good, bad or risky something is
"First she had to assess the risk of each part."
Alternative -- another option you can choose instead
"The alternative was to test everything by hand, but that was too slow."
Reasonable -- fair and sensible
"It was a reasonable thing to leave for later."
To reconsider -- to think again about a decision
"If the risk grew, she would reconsider the plan."
Cost-effective -- giving good value for the time or money spent
"Automated tests were more cost-effective, so she chose them."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Trade-off
Hypothetical
Contingency
To mitigate
Downside
To postpone
To justify
To assess
Alternative
Reasonable
To reconsider
Cost-effective

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

With only two days to test, Ana planned in hypotheticals. She asked herself, "If I had more time, what would I automate first?" — an imagined situation, not a real one. She made a trade-off: "If we tested everything by hand, we would miss the deadline," so she chose the cost-effective option. She assessed the low-risk screen and said, "If it failed, nobody would lose money," so postponing it was reasonable. And she built a contingency: "If the feature failed badly, we would roll back." The past forms here do not mean the past — they mark the situation as imagined.

1. When Ana says "If I had more time, I would automate first", is she talking about the real past?
A No -- it is an imagined situation; the past form marks it as hypothetical.
B Yes -- it describes something that really happened yesterday.
C It is a command to the team.
2. "If it failed, nobody would lose money" is formed with:
A will + base verb in both parts.
B If + past simple (failed), and would + base verb (would lose).
C the present continuous.
3. Why does Ana use the second conditional to plan?
A To report a bug that was already found.
B To weigh imagined situations and decide what she would do.
C To describe a routine she does every day.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Second conditional = If + past simple, would + base verb. Use it for imagined or unlikely situations, and to weigh options before you decide.

TypeFormExample
AffirmativeIf + past simple, would + base verbIf I had more time, I would automate the checkout.
Negativewould not (wouldn't) + base verbIf it failed, nobody would lose money and we would not panic.
QuestionWhat would + subject + base verb?What would you do if the tests failed on Friday?
Common mistakeDo not use "will" in the if-part: not "If I will have time" — say "If I had time, I would..." The past form marks the idea as imagined.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"If I had more time, I the checkout tests first."
"It is a between speed and safety."
"We could the low-risk tests to the next sprint."
"To the risk, we would keep the old version ready."
"Automated tests were more , so she chose them."
"If the reporting screen failed, nobody money."

Stage 2: Put the Plan in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's test plan in the correct order.

?She built a contingency plan in case the tests failed on Friday.
?She asked herself a hypothetical: if she had more time, what would she automate first?
?She kept the old version ready, so if the feature failed badly, they would roll back.
?She made a trade-off and chose automated tests for the checkout, because they were more cost-effective.
?She assessed the risk of the other screens and postponed the low-risk one to the next sprint.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If I had more time, I would automate the checkout tests first.
It is a trade-off between speed and safety.
If the reporting screen failed, nobody would lose money.
To mitigate the risk, we would keep the old version ready.
If the feature failed badly, we would roll back and ship later.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each decision-making situation.

A stakeholder asks what you would test first if you only had one day. You answer:
A "If I only had one day, I would test the payment flow first."
B "If I will have one day, I test everything."
C "I tested the payment flow last year."
You want to justify postponing a low-risk screen. The best line is:
A "I do not want to test it, so I will not."
B "If that screen failed, nobody would lose money, so it is reasonable to postpone it."
C "It is broken, so we cannot test it."
You describe your contingency for Friday. You say:
A "If the tests found a serious bug, we would postpone the release by a few hours."
B "It will be fine, do not worry about it."
