C "We found a serious bug and shipped anyway."
You explain a choice between two options that each have a cost. You are describing a:
A downtime.
B trade-off.
C patch.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

You have only two days to test a big new feature before a release. In one minute, explain your plan using the second conditional: say what you would automate first, one trade-off you would make, one screen you would postpone and why, and your contingency if the tests failed on Friday. Keep it calm and reasonable.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1If I had more time, I would automate the checkout tests first.
2It is a trade-off between speed and safety.
3If the reporting screen failed, nobody would lose money.
4To mitigate the risk, we would keep the old version ready.
5If the feature failed badly, we would roll back and ship later.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Used To, Be Used To, Get Used To -- Change and Adaptation

Ana joins a fully remote team and talks with her new team lead about the transition: what she used to do, what she is used to now, and what she is still getting used to. Key words: adaptation, transition, to adjust, mindset, comfort zone, to embrace, unfamiliar, gradual, to settle in, resilience, shift, to thrive. Structure: used to / be used to / get used to.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Adaptation -- the process of changing to fit a new situation
"Every big change needs a period of adaptation."
Transition -- a change from one situation, job, or state to another
"The transition to remote work took a few weeks."
To adjust -- to change slightly to fit a new situation
"It took time to adjust to the new schedule."
Mindset -- a person's usual way of thinking and attitude
"A growth mindset helps you learn from mistakes."
Comfort zone -- the familiar situation where you feel safe and relaxed
"Learning English pushes me out of my comfort zone."
To embrace -- to accept something new willingly and with enthusiasm
"Good teams embrace change instead of fearing it."
Unfamiliar -- not known to you; new and a little strange
"The new tools felt unfamiliar at first."
Gradual -- happening slowly, in small steps
"Getting used to a new team is a gradual process."
To settle in -- to become comfortable in a new place or role
"It took a month to settle in at the new company."
Resilience -- the ability to recover quickly from difficulty
"Resilience helps you keep going when work is hard."
Shift -- a change in position, focus, or direction
"There was a big shift in how we work after 2020."
To thrive -- to grow and do very well
"Some people thrive when everything changes."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Adaptation
Transition
To adjust
Mindset
Comfort zone
To embrace
Unfamiliar
Gradual
To settle in
Resilience
Shift
To thrive

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana just joined a new, fully remote team. It is a big transition. She used to work in an office every day, so the silence at home is still unfamiliar. On her first week, almost everything is outside her comfort zone. But her mindset is good: she knows nobody is used to a new routine on day one. Adaptation is a gradual process. Little by little, she is getting used to writing things down instead of just talking. She used to hate video calls, but now she does not mind them. With a bit of resilience, she is starting to thrive. Notice the pattern: used to + base verb is a past habit ("I used to work"), while be/get used to + -ing means being or becoming accustomed ("I am used to working").

1. Which sentence describes a past habit correctly?
A "I am used to work in an office."
B "I used to work in an office."
C "I use to work in an office."
2. After "be used to" and "get used to", the verb is in the...
A -ing form (or a noun).
B base form, with no ending.
C past simple.
3. "I am getting used to the new tools" means...
A I am in the process of becoming comfortable with them.
B I used them a lot in the past.
C I will never be comfortable with them.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Used To / Be Used To / Get Used To

GRAMMAR

How to talk about a past habit, a state you are comfortable with, and a change in progress.

StructureMeaningForm & Example
used toa past habit, not true nowused to + base verb — I used to work in an office.
be used toalready accustomed (a state)be used to + noun / -ing — I am used to working alone.
get used tobecoming accustomed (a process)get used to + noun / -ing — I got used to the routine.
Negative (past)did not use toI didn't use to like video calls. (no -d after "did not")
Key ruleAfter used to (past habit) use the base verb. After be/get used to use a noun or -ing — never "I am used to work".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The move to remote work was a big for the whole team."
"A new job always pushes you out of your ."
"It took me a few weeks to to the new schedule."
"With a bit of , you recover quickly when work is hard."
"Getting used to a new team is a process."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana settled into her new remote team, then put the steps in the correct order.

?After that, she asked a teammate how they got used to the routine.
?First, she accepted that the first weeks would feel unfamiliar.
?Finally, after a month, she felt at home and started to thrive.
?Then, she picked one new habit to adjust to each week.
?Next, she practiced the new tools a little every day.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I used to work in an office, but now I work from home.
I am used to working remotely now.
I am still getting used to the new tools.
It took me a month to settle in at the new company.
I did not use to like video calls, but now I do.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

A colleague asks how you worked before the pandemic. You say:
A "I used to work in the office five days a week."
B "I use to work in the office five days a week."
C "I am used to work in the office five days a week."
You want to say remote work feels normal to you now. You say:
A "I used to working remotely."
B "I am used to working remotely."
C "I am used to work remotely."
You want to say you are still adapting to the new stand-up format. You say:
A "I am still getting used to the new stand-up format."
B "I am still get used to the new stand-up format."
C "I still used to the new stand-up format."
You want to say you did not like async work in the past. You say:
A "I didn't use to like async work."
B "I didn't used to like async work."
C "I don't use to like async work."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a real change in your career or work life. Describe it using all three structures: how you used to work, what you are used to now, and what you are still getting used to. Then say what helped you adapt. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1I used to work in an office, but now I work from home.
2I am used to working remotely now.
3I am still getting used to the new tools.
4It took me a month to settle in, but I feel at home now.
5Change is a gradual process, so I give myself time.
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Advanced Modals -- Would Rather, Had Better, May As Well

Ana's remote team must choose a testing tool for a new module. She writes a recommendation: her preference, her warning, and her pragmatic call. New words: preference, to recommend, advisable, worthwhile, to opt for, viable, upside, rationale, reluctant, to lean toward, prudent, compelling. Grammar: advanced modals for recommending and preferring (I would rather use the free tool; we had better decide this week; we may as well set it up now).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the words for making a strong recommendation. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Preference -- a thing you like or want more than another
"My preference is the open-source tool."
To recommend -- to advise something as the best choice
"I would recommend the free tool for our team."
Advisable -- wise or sensible to do
"It is advisable to decide before the deadline."
Worthwhile -- worth the time, money or effort
"Setting up the tool now is worthwhile."
To opt for -- to choose one option instead of others
"In the end, the team opted for the open-source tool."
Viable -- realistic and able to work as a choice
"Both tools are viable, but one is faster to set up."
Upside -- the positive part or advantage of a choice
"The upside of the free tool is that everyone knows it."
Rationale -- the reasons behind a decision
"Let me explain the rationale for my recommendation."
Reluctant -- not wanting to do something; hesitant
"Some teammates were reluctant to switch tools."
To lean toward -- to prefer or be inclined to one option
"I am leaning toward the open-source option."
Prudent -- careful and sensible; avoiding risk
"It was more prudent to pick a good-enough tool now."
Compelling -- strong enough to convince you
"She made a compelling case for the free tool."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Preference
To recommend
Advisable
Worthwhile
To opt for
Viable
Upside
Rationale
Reluctant
To lean toward
Prudent
Compelling

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Ana had to recommend a testing tool, so she used three modals to be clear. To share her preference, she wrote, "I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one" — would rather + base verb, with no "to". To warn the team about the deadline, she added, "We had better decide this week" — strong advice, almost a warning. And because the team was already blocked, she suggested, "We may as well set up the tool now" — there was no better option, so why not. All three take the base verb: would rather use, had better decide, may as well set up.

1. When Ana says "I would rather use the open-source tool", she is expressing:
A a preference -- what she likes more.
B a command to the team.
C a report about a past decision.
2. "We had better decide this week" is used to:
A describe a daily routine.
B give firm advice, almost a warning.
C ask a polite question.
3. What verb form comes after would rather, had better and may as well?
A to + verb (to use, to decide).
B the base verb with no "to" (use, decide, set up).
C the -ing form (using, deciding).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Advanced Modals

GRAMMAR

Three modals for recommending and preferring. All take the base verb with no "to".

ModalUse / FormExample
would ratherpreference: would rather + base verb (than...)I would rather use the free tool than the paid one.
had betterstrong advice / warning: had better + base verbWe had better decide this week, or we will miss the deadline.
may as wellno better option: may as well + base verbWe are blocked, so we may as well set up the tool now.
Common mistakeDo not add "to": not "would rather to use" — say "would rather use". And it is had better, never "would better".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"I would rather the open-source tool than the paid one."
"We better decide this week, or we will miss the deadline."
"We are blocked anyway, so we may as well up the tool now."
"The is that the whole team already knows the tool."
"It is to start early rather than wait."
"She made a case for the free tool."

Stage 2: Put the Recommendation in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's recommendation in the correct order.

?She warned the team that they had better decide this week, before the deadline.
?She compared the two viable tools and formed a clear preference for the open-source one.
?Since the team was blocked, she suggested they may as well set up the tool now.
?She wrote her rationale, explaining the upside and making a compelling case.
?She was honest about the downside, the weaker support, so the team could trust her.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one.
We had better decide this week, or we will miss the deadline.
We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now.
The upside is that the whole team already knows it.
It is advisable to start early rather than wait.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each recommendation situation.

A teammate asks which tool you prefer. You answer:
A "I would rather use the free tool, because the team already knows it."
B "I would rather to use the free tool."
C "I used the free tool last year."
The deadline is close and nobody has decided. The best warning is:
A "We would better decide this week."
B "We had better decide this week, or we will miss the deadline."
C "There is no hurry, we can decide anytime."
The team is blocked, waiting on an API. You suggest a good use of the dead time:
A "We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set up the tool now."
B "We are blocked, so let us do nothing."
C "We may as well to wait for the API."
You want your recommendation to be trusted. The most compelling line is:
A "Just trust me, it is the best tool."
B "The upside is fast setup; the downside is less support -- but it is the more prudent choice for us."
C "There are no downsides at all, it is perfect."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Your team must choose between two tools for a new project. In one minute, recommend one: say what you would rather use and why (the upside), warn the team with "had better" about the deadline, and add a "may as well" suggestion for a moment of dead time. Be honest about one downside. Keep it clear and reasonable.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1I would rather use the open-source tool than the paid one.
2We had better decide this week, or we will miss the deadline.
3We are blocked anyway, so we may as well set it up now.
4The upside is that the whole team already knows it.
5It is advisable to start early rather than wait.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Hedging Language -- Sounding Measured and Diplomatically Precise

Ana joins an international release standup and must flag a risky bug and question the ship date without sounding blunt. She softens her claims and qualifies her opinions using hedging language. Key words: to suggest, tentative, apparently, presumably, to imply, cautious, seemingly, to downplay, roughly, to soften, understated, to qualify. Structure: hedging language (softening opinions and claims).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

To suggest -- to hint that something is probably true, without stating it directly
"The logs suggest a timeout, but it is not confirmed."
Tentative -- not final or certain; said or done with caution
"I only have a tentative answer for now."
Apparently -- based on what seems true, though not fully confirmed
"Apparently the bug only happens under heavy load."
Presumably -- most likely; used when you assume something is true
"Presumably the test will take about two hours."
To imply -- to communicate something indirectly, without saying it openly
"I did not say no; I implied we needed more time."
Cautious -- careful to avoid mistakes or risks; not rushing to conclusions
"I am cautious about promising a fix today."
Seemingly -- in a way that appears to be true, on the surface
"A seemingly small change broke the login."
To downplay -- to make something seem less important or serious than it is
"Do not downplay the risk just to sound positive."
Roughly -- approximately; not exactly
"It will take roughly two hours to test."
To soften -- to make a message gentler or less direct
"I softened my email so it did not sound like a complaint."
Understated -- expressed in a calm, restrained way, without exaggeration
"Her understated reply hid how serious the issue was."
To qualify -- to add a detail that limits a statement and makes it less absolute
"Let me qualify that: it works, but only on the latest version."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

To suggest
Tentative
Apparently
Presumably
To imply
Cautious
Seemingly
To downplay
Roughly
To soften
Understated
To qualify

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In her release standup, Ana wants to flag a risk without sounding blunt, so she softens what she says. Instead of "this is a bug", she says it seemingly only happens under heavy load, and the logs suggest a timeout. She is cautious, so she keeps her view tentative: "I am not sure yet." When her manager asks how long a test takes, she does not pretend to be exact; she says roughly two hours, and presumably they will know by the afternoon. She does not downplay the risk to look positive, and she does not overstate it either. When she disagrees, she does not say "you are wrong"; she softens it to "I'm not sure that's quite right." Notice the pattern: a modal (might, could), a reporting verb (seem, suggest), a cautious adverb (apparently, roughly), or a tentative opener (I'd say) turns a hard claim into a measured one. One hedge is usually enough; too many and you sound unsure.

1. Which sentence sounds the most measured and diplomatic?
A "This is a bug and the release will fail."
B "This might be a bug, so it could be worth another test."
C "You are wrong, the release will definitely fail."
2. How many hedges does the text recommend per sentence?
A Usually one; too many sound unsure.
B As many as possible, to be safe.
C None; hedging is always weak.
3. To disagree diplomatically, Ana says...
A "I'm not sure that's quite right."
B "You are completely wrong."
C "That is impossible."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Hedging Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Four ways to soften a claim or an opinion so you sound measured, not blunt.

DeviceFunctionExample
Modal: might / could / maysoften a claim you are not sure ofThis might be a regression.
seem / appear / suggest / tend toreport cautiously, not as factThe logs suggest a timeout.
Adverbs: apparently / presumably / roughly / seeminglyadd caution to a statementIt is apparently intermittent.
Tentative openers: I'd say / it could be worthsoften an opinion or suggestionI'd say we hold the release.
Key ruleOne hedge per sentence is usually enough. Modals do not stack (never might can). To disagree, soften instead of contradicting: I'm not sure that's quite right.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"The logs a timeout, but it is not confirmed."
"I only have a answer for now."
"It will take two hours to run the test."
"Let me that: it works, but only on the latest version."
"Please do not the risk just to sound positive."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana raised her concern without sounding blunt, then put the steps in the correct order.

?After that, she suggested one more load test instead of demanding a delay.
?First, she checked the logs and formed a tentative view of the problem.
?Finally, she let the team decide, and they agreed to hold the release.
?Then, she softened her message so it would not sound like an accusation.
?Next, she gave a rough estimate of how long the test would take.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I would say we are mostly there, but there is one risk I would like to flag.
The logs seem to suggest an intermittent timeout.
I am not sure that is quite right about the root cause.
Apparently, the issue only happens under heavy load.
It would take roughly two hours, presumably.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

A teammate asks if the feature is ready. You have a small doubt. You say:
A "I'd say we're mostly there, but there's one risk I'd like to flag."
B "It is broken and we cannot ship it."
C "Everything is perfect, ship it now."
You are not certain the bug is a timeout. You report it as:
A "The logs prove it is a timeout."
B "The logs seem to suggest a timeout, but it is not confirmed."
C "It might can be a timeout."
You disagree with a colleague's root cause. You want to keep the conversation open. You say:
A "I'm not sure that's quite right about the root cause."
B "No, you are completely wrong."
C "That is impossible."
Someone asks how long a test takes. You are not exact. You say:
A "Roughly two hours, presumably."
B "Exactly one hundred and seventeen minutes."
C "I have no idea at all."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real risk or doubt you would need to raise at work. In an international standup, flag it diplomatically: report it cautiously (seem / suggest), qualify how sure you are (might / apparently), and suggest a next step without demanding it. Use at least three hedges. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I'd say we're mostly there, but there's one risk I'd like to flag.
2The logs seem to suggest an intermittent issue.
3I wouldn't put it that strongly; it might be worth another test.
4I'm not sure that's quite right, to be honest.
5Roughly speaking, it would take about two hours.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

Third & Mixed Conditionals -- Rewriting Outcomes in Postmortems

A bad deploy caused a two-hour outage. Ana runs a blameless postmortem and rewrites the outcome with conditionals. New words: postmortem, retrospective, hindsight, incident, outage, blameless, preventable, takeaway, oversight, near miss, contributing factor, to backfire. Grammar: third conditional (if we had tested it, the outage would not have happened) and mixed conditionals (if we had documented it, we would not be here now).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the words for looking back at an incident. Tap Listen to hear each one.

Postmortem -- a review after an incident to learn from it
"We ran a postmortem the day after the outage."
Retrospective -- a meeting to look back on what went well and badly
"In the retrospective, the team agreed on three takeaways."
Hindsight -- understanding a situation only after it has happened
"In hindsight, the warning signs were all there."
Incident -- an unplanned event that disrupts a service
"A bad deploy caused a serious incident."
Outage -- a period when a system is down and unavailable
"The outage lasted two hours, and users could not log in."
Blameless -- focused on causes, not on blaming people
"This is a blameless meeting: we look at causes, not people."
Preventable -- able to have been avoided
"The bug was completely preventable."
Takeaway -- a key lesson or point to remember
"The main takeaway is to test every login change."
Oversight -- an unintentional failure to notice or do something
"The switched-off alert was a small oversight."
Near miss -- an event that almost caused harm but did not
"A near miss last sprint was never written down."
Contributing factor -- one of several causes that led to a problem
"The old alert was a contributing factor, not the root cause."
To backfire -- to have the opposite effect to what was intended
"A postmortem can backfire if it turns into blame."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Postmortem
Retrospective
Hindsight
Incident
Outage
Blameless
Preventable
Takeaway
Oversight
Near miss
Contributing factor
To backfire

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After the outage, Ana wrote a postmortem. To look back at the past, she used the third conditional: "If we had tested the login flow in staging, the outage would not have happened" — if + past perfect, would have + past participle. To connect that past mistake to the present, she used a mixed conditional: "If we had documented the near miss, we would not be here now" — a past cause with a present result. And to show it was not just bad luck, she used another mixed form: "If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner" — a present truth with a past result.

1. "If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened" is:
A a third conditional about the past -- what did not happen.
B a plan for next week.
C a simple present routine.
2. "If we had documented the near miss, we would not be here now" connects:
A two future events.
B a past cause with a present result (a mixed conditional).
C two present habits.
3. What verb form comes after "if" in the third conditional?
A will + verb (if we will test).
B the past perfect: had + past participle (if we had tested).
C the -ing form (if we testing).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Third & Mixed Conditionals

GRAMMAR

All of these use if and a would form. Only the tenses move.

ConditionalUse / FormExample
Thirda past that did not happen: if + past perfect, would have + past participleIf we had tested it, the outage would not have happened.
Mixed (past → present)past cause, present result: if + past perfect, would + base verbIf we had documented it, we would not be here now.
Mixed (present → past)present truth, past result: if + past simple, would have + past participleIf our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.
Common mistakeDo not put a would form after "if": not "if we would have tested" — say "if we had tested". The would goes in the RESULT, never in the if-clause.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not happened."
"If we documented the near miss, we would not be here now."
"In , that oversight was a contributing factor."
"The bug was completely , so we treat it as one."
"This is a postmortem: we look at causes, not at people."
"A postmortem can if it turns into blame."

Stage 2: Put the Postmortem in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of Ana's postmortem in the correct order.

?After that, she used hindsight to see what the team would have done differently.
?First, she wrote a factual timeline of the incident, from the first alert to the fix.
?Finally, she shared the key takeaways in a calm, blameless retrospective.
?Then she found the root cause and the contributing factors, without blaming anyone.
?Next, she agreed on preventable actions so the same outage would not happen again.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened.
If we had documented the near miss, we would not be dealing with it now.
If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.
This is a blameless postmortem: we look at causes, not at people.
In hindsight, that oversight was a contributing factor.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each postmortem situation.

A bug reached production because it was never tested. Look back with a third conditional:
A "If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened."
B "If we would have tested it, the outage stops."
C "If we test it, the outage did not happen."
A past mistake is still causing problems today. The best mixed conditional is:
A "If we had logged the near miss, we would not be dealing with it now."
B "If we log it, we will be fine."
C "If we had logged it, we would not have a problem yesterday."
You want the postmortem to stay constructive. You say:
A "Whose fault was this outage?"
B "This is a blameless review; let us look at the causes, not the people."
C "Someone here should have been more careful."
Your monitoring is generally weak, and it let a past bug slip. The best line is:
A "If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner."
B "If our monitoring is better, we catch everything."
C "If our monitoring were better, we catch it sooner."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real or imagined incident at work: something that broke, a deadline you missed, or a bug that reached production. In one minute, run a mini blameless postmortem out loud. Use one third conditional (if we had ..., the ... would not have happened) and one mixed conditional to connect the past to the present. End with one clear, preventable takeaway.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1If we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened.
2If we had documented the near miss, we would not be dealing with it now.
3If our monitoring were better, we would have caught it sooner.
4This is a blameless postmortem: we look at causes, not at people.
5In hindsight, that oversight was a contributing factor.
Lesson 17 -- Pre-class

Reported Speech -- Capturing What Was Said in Meetings and Escalations

The morning after a night incident, Ana debriefs her manager on the escalation call: she reports what the client, QA and the on-call engineer said, shifting the tenses back and keeping the exact wording where it matters. Key words: to relay, to convey, to reiterate, to paraphrase, to notify, to acknowledge, recipient, thread, chain of command, discrepancy, wording, verbatim. Structure: reported speech (backshift across tenses).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

To relay -- to pass a message from one person to another
"I will relay the client's decision to the release manager."
To convey -- to communicate an idea or feeling so others clearly understand it
"I could not convey how urgent the outage really was."
To reiterate -- to say something again to make it clear or to stress it
"Let me reiterate the one decision we made on the call."
To paraphrase -- to restate what someone said in your own words
"I will paraphrase the long explanation, but keep the numbers exact."
To notify -- to formally tell someone that something has happened
"We must notify the incident manager before we close the ticket."
To acknowledge -- to confirm that you have received or noticed something
"Please acknowledge the alert so we know you saw it."
Recipient -- the person who receives a message or email
"The recipient of the report is the release manager."
Thread -- a connected series of messages or emails on one topic
"The whole decision is buried in a long email thread."
Chain of command -- the order in which people report to and answer to each other
"This issue has to go up the chain of command."
Discrepancy -- a difference between two accounts or numbers that should match
"There is a discrepancy between what he said and the logs."
Wording -- the specific choice of words used in a message
"The wording of the alert was confusing, so nobody acted."
Verbatim -- using exactly the same words; word for word
"I copied her commitment verbatim into the ticket."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

To relay
To convey
To reiterate
To paraphrase
To notify
To acknowledge
Recipient
Thread
Chain of command
Discrepancy
Wording
Verbatim

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The morning after the outage, Ana had to relay to her manager what everyone had said on the escalation call. She was careful, because a report is only useful if it is faithful. On the call, Priya had said, "the fix is live", so Ana reported that Priya said the fix was live. David had said, "we deployed a bad config last night", so Ana reported that David said they had deployed a bad config the night before. Notice the pattern: when you report speech, the tense usually shifts one step back, and time words move too: today becomes that day, yesterday becomes the day before, will becomes would, can becomes could. The client had asked, "can you send a summary today?", so Ana reported that the client asked whether we could send a summary that day, with no question mark and normal word order. She used a clean paraphrase for the long parts, but she kept the client's commitment verbatim, because the recipient on the incident thread would act on it. When she spotted a discrepancy between what someone said and the logs, she flagged it instead of hiding it. That is what a good report does: it shifts the tense, keeps the wording honest, and sends the key facts up the chain.

1. Priya said, "the fix is live." How does Ana report it?
A Priya said the fix is live.
B Priya said the fix was live.
C Priya says the fix will be live.
2. The client asked, "can you send a summary today?" How does Ana report it?
A The client asked whether we could send a summary that day.
B The client asked can we send a summary today?
C The client asked if we can send a summary today.
3. Why does Ana keep the client's commitment verbatim?
A Because the recipient will act on the exact words.
B Because paraphrasing is always wrong.
C Because verbatim reports do not need a backshift.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Backshift Rule

GRAMMAR

When you report what someone said, the tense usually moves one step back.

Direct speechReported speechShift
"It is down."She said it was down.present → past
"We deployed it."He said they had deployed it.past → past perfect
"I will fix it."She said she would fix it.will → would
"Can you help?"He asked if I could help.can → could
Key ruleStatements: said (that). Questions: asked if / whether + normal word order, no question mark. Use tell with a person (told me), say without one. Time words shift too: today → that day, yesterday → the day before, tomorrow → the next day.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"She said the login service down for about forty minutes."
"He said they deployed a bad config the night before."
"The client asked we could send a summary that day."
"Let me their exact words verbatim."
"There is a between what he said and the logs."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana reported the escalation call to her manager, then put the steps in the correct order.

?After that, she paraphrased the long explanations but kept the key numbers and commitments verbatim.
?First, she listened carefully and noted the exact wording of what each person had said.
?Finally, she relayed the clean summary to the recipients on the incident thread.
?Then, she shifted the tenses back so the whole report was in reported speech.
?Next, she checked for any discrepancy between what people said and what the logs showed.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

She said the login service was down for about forty minutes.
He said they had deployed a bad config the night before.
The client asked whether we could send a summary that day.
Let me relay their exact words verbatim.
This needs to go up the chain of command.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

On the call, Priya said, "the service is slow." You report it to your manager as:
A "Priya said the service was slow."
B "Priya said the service is slow."
C "Priya says the service will be slow."
The client asked, "can you confirm it in writing?" You report the question as:
A "The client asked can we confirm it in writing?"
B "The client asked whether we could confirm it in writing."
C "The client asked if we can confirm it in writing?"
You want to say that David gave you a specific instruction. You choose:
A "David told me to relay the client's words verbatim."
B "David said me to relay the client's words verbatim."
C "David told to relay the client's words verbatim."
A key commitment must reach the right people. You say:
A "I will notify the recipients on the thread and send it up the chain of command."
B "I will keep it to myself for now."
C "I will paraphrase the exact numbers so they sound better."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real call or meeting you had to report to someone at work. Debrief it in reported speech: report what two or three people said, shift the tenses back, keep one commitment verbatim, and say who you would notify. Use at least three reporting verbs. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1She said the service was down for about forty minutes.
2He said they had deployed a bad config the night before.
3The client asked whether we could send a summary that day.
4Let me relay their exact words verbatim.
5This needs to go up the chain of command.
Lesson 18 -- Pre-class

Speaking Without a Script -- Holding the Floor and Staying Fluent

A live client call goes off-script. Ana cannot freeze, so she uses fluency strategies to hold the floor and keep talking. New words: to hold the floor, to stall, train of thought, to backtrack, to digress, tangent, to elaborate, to reword, to improvise, to signpost, to trail off, to ramble. Focus: fluency strategies -- stalling to buy time, holding the floor, signposting, and paraphrasing when the exact word will not come.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Learn the strategies for speaking without a script. Tap Listen to hear each one.

To hold the floor -- to keep control of the conversation and your turn to speak
"When the questions came fast, Ana held the floor and finished her point."
To stall -- to slow down on purpose to buy time before you answer
"She stalled for a second: ‘That’s a great question, let me think.’"
Train of thought -- the line of thinking you are following as you speak
"The interruption made her lose her train of thought."
To backtrack -- to go back and correct or restate what you just said
"Let me backtrack: I said Tuesday, but the fix ships on Thursday."
To digress -- to move away from the main topic while you are speaking
"I don’t want to digress, so let me get back to the release."
Tangent -- a related but off-topic point you drift onto
"He went off on a tangent about the old system."
To elaborate -- to add more detail to what you have said
"Could you elaborate on the root cause a little more?"
To reword -- to say the same thing using different words
"If they don’t understand, I reword it in plainer English."
To improvise -- to speak without preparation, thinking on your feet
"The demo broke, so Ana had to improvise."
To signpost -- to use small phrases that guide the listener through your answer
"She signposts her answer: ‘First…, and second…’"
To trail off -- to gradually stop speaking without finishing your sentence
"Under pressure, her voice trailed off mid-sentence."
To ramble -- to talk on and on in an aimless, unfocused way
"When she is nervous, she rambles instead of getting to the point."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

To hold the floor
To stall
Train of thought
To backtrack
To digress
Tangent
To elaborate
To reword
To improvise
To signpost
To trail off
To ramble

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When the client asked something off-script, Ana did not freeze. To buy a second, she used a stalling phrase: "That’s a great question, let me think for a moment." To keep her turn when someone jumped in, she held the floor: "If I can just finish this point, then I’ll come to that." To help the client follow her, she signposted her answer: "There are two parts to this. First…, and second…" And when the exact word would not come, she reworded the idea instead of stopping: "In other words, what I mean is the fix is low-risk." None of it was scripted — the phrases simply kept her talking and in control.

1. Why does Ana say "That’s a great question, let me think for a moment"?
A to stall and buy a second before she answers.
B to end the call politely.
C to ask the client to repeat the question.
2. "There are two parts to this. First…, and second…" is an example of:
A rambling without a point.
B signposting, to guide the listener through the answer.
C backtracking to correct a mistake.
3. When the exact word will not come, what does Ana do?
A she stops the call to look it up.
B she lets her voice trail off.
C she rewords the idea in plainer English.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Fluency Strategies

GRAMMAR

Fluency is not about knowing every word. It is a set of ready-made phrases that keep you talking and in control.

StrategyWhen to use itPhrase
Stall (buy time)a surprise question, your mind goes blank"That’s a great question, let me think for a moment."
Hold the floorsomeone interrupts before you finish"If I can just finish this point, then I’ll come to that."
Signpostyour answer has more than one part"There are two parts to this. First…, and second…"
Rewordthe exact word will not come"In other words, what I mean is…"
Common mistakeDo not fill the pause by rambling or letting your voice trail off. Use a purposeful phrase to stall or signpost, then continue.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"When the call went off-script, Ana did not freeze; she managed to the floor."
"To buy a second, she and said, ‘That’s a great question.’"
"There are two parts to this, so let me my answer."
"If the exact term will not come, I just the idea in plainer English."
"I don’t want to , so let me come back to the release."
"Sorry, let me : the fix ships on Thursday, not Tuesday."

Stage 2: Put the Call in Order

Order

Listen to the story, then put the steps of how Ana handled the off-script question in the correct order.

?Next, when Greg pulled her onto a tangent, she got back on track and returned to the release.
?First, she stalled for a second and said, "That’s a great question, let me think for a moment."
?Finally, she noticed a wrong date, backtracked cleanly, and corrected it without losing her train of thought.
?Then she signposted her answer, telling Greg there were two parts to it.
?After that, she reworded the idea in plainer English when the exact term would not come.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

That's a great question, let me think for a moment.
If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to that.
There are two parts to this. First the bug, and second the timeline.
In other words, what I mean is the fix is low-risk.
I don't want to digress, so let me come back to the release.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each live-call situation.

A client asks a question you did not expect and your mind goes blank. You say:
A "That's a great question -- let me think for a moment."
B "Um... I don't know... maybe... it's hard to say..."
C [say nothing and wait]
A teammate keeps interrupting, but you need to finish your point. You say:
A "If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to your question."
B "Fine, forget what I was saying."
C "Please stop talking over me."
You just realized you gave the wrong date. The best move is to:
A ignore it and hope nobody noticed.
B backtrack cleanly: "Sorry, let me rephrase that -- the fix ships on Thursday."
C ramble about the calendar until you forget the point.
You cannot remember the exact technical term mid-sentence. You:
A reword it: "In other words, it only affects the login screen."
B let your voice trail off and change the subject.
C stop the call to search for the word.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are on a live client call and someone asks a question you did not prepare for. In one minute, improvise your answer out loud. Stall for a second to buy time, signpost your answer in two parts, and reword at least once when a word will not come. If you drift onto a tangent, get back on track.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1That's a great question, let me think for a moment.
2If I can just finish this point, then I'll come to that.
3There are two parts to this. First the bug, and second the timeline.
4In other words, what I mean is the fix is low-risk.
5Sorry, let me rephrase that -- the fix ships on Thursday.
Lesson 19 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Milestone -- Progress Review and Fluency Benchmark

Halfway through the program, Ana reviews the first half: the key words she has collected across QA, incidents and meetings, and the structures she can now speak with confidence -- conditionals, reported speech and diplomatic modals. This is a review, not new material. Review words: requirement, to escalate, root cause, blocker, to follow up, edge case, to deploy, outage, trade-off, to mitigate, to reiterate, to signpost. Structures reviewed: conditionals, reported speech, modals.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

A quick review of twelve words from the first half. Listen to each one and read the example.

Requirement -- something the system must do, agreed with the client
"Each requirement is a small promise to the client."
To escalate -- to pass a serious issue to a higher level or team
"If it blocks the release, we escalate it right away."
Root cause -- the real, underlying reason for a problem
"The root cause was a bad config, not the network."
Blocker -- something that stops you from making progress on a task
"My only blocker is that the new build is not ready."
To follow up -- to check on something or continue it after a first contact
"I will follow up with the dev team after the review."
Edge case -- an unusual situation at the limit of normal use
"As an edge case, I test a password with 200 characters."
To deploy -- to release software so that users can use it
"We deploy the new version every two weeks."
Outage -- a period when a system is down and unavailable
"The outage lasted two hours, and users could not log in."
Trade-off -- a balance between two good things you cannot both fully have
"It is a trade-off between speed and safety."
To mitigate -- to make a risk or a problem less serious
"To mitigate the risk, she kept the old version ready."
To reiterate -- to say something again to make it clear or to stress it
"Let me reiterate the one decision we made on the call."
To signpost -- to use small phrases that guide the listener through your answer
"She signposts her answer: first this, then that."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Requirement
To escalate
Root cause
Blocker
To follow up
Edge case
To deploy
Outage
Trade-off
To mitigate
To reiterate
To signpost

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Halfway through the program, Ana prepared for her midpoint review by looking back at the structures she now owned. She thought about a recent outage. On the call, the on-call engineer had said, "the fix is live", so Ana could report that he said the fix was live, shifting the tense one step back. She could also describe what almost happened: "if we had not caught the discrepancy, the root cause would have stayed hidden, and the outage would have been worse." That is a third conditional -- an unreal past, with had + past participle in the if clause and would have + past participle in the result. And when she disagreed with a plan, she no longer sounded blunt: instead of "we must not deploy on Friday", she softened it with a modal -- "it might be worth waiting, and if I were you, I would wait until Monday." None of this was new tonight; it was a review. The point of the midpoint is not to learn more words, but to use the words and structures you already have -- fluently, without stopping to build each sentence.

1. The engineer said, "the fix is live." How does Ana report it?
A He said the fix is live.
B He said the fix was live.
C He says the fix will be live.
2. Which sentence is a correct third conditional?
A If we would have caught it, the outage would not happen.
B If we had caught it, the outage would not have happened.
C If we catch it, the outage would not have happened.
3. What is the point of the midpoint review, according to the text?
A To use the words and structures she already has, fluently.
B To learn as many brand-new words as possible.
C To stop using conditionals and reported speech.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Structures Reviewed

GRAMMAR

The three structures from the first half, side by side. You already own them -- the goal is to use them fluently.

StructurePatternExample
Third conditionalIf + had + past participle, would have + past participleIf we had tested it, the outage would not have happened.
Reported speechsaid (that) + backshift: is → was, will → wouldHe said the root cause was a bad config.
Diplomatic modalsmight / could / would + verb; "it might be worth..."It might be worth rolling back tonight.
Key ruleIn the third conditional, never use "would have" in the if clause. Reported speech needs the backshift. Modals take the bare verb: "should escalate", not "should to escalate".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the whole sentence.

"He said the root cause a bad config."
"If we tested that edge case, the outage would have been smaller."
"The client asked we could deploy that day."
"It might be worth back, rather than shipping tonight."
"I will up on that and get back to you."

Stage 2: Put the Steps in Order

Order

Listen to how Ana prepared for her midpoint review, then put the steps in the correct order.

?After that, she practiced telling each story out loud, without a script, until it felt natural.
?First, she looked back through her notes and collected the key words she had learned.
?Finally, she walked into the benchmark call calmly, ready to show how far her English had come.
?Then, she picked one real story for each structure: a conditional, a piece of reported speech, and a diplomatic recommendation.
?Next, she wrote down the two areas she still wanted to improve in the second half.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If we had caught it sooner, the outage would have been shorter.
She said the root cause was a bad config.
It might be worth rolling back, rather than shipping tonight.
Let me signpost my answer: there are two parts to this.
I will follow up on that and get back to you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real work situation.

On the call, Priya said, "the service is slow." You report it to your manager as:
A "Priya said the service was slow."
B "Priya said the service is slow."
C "Priya says the service will be slow."
You want to describe a past mistake as an unreal past. You say:
A "If we would have tested the edge case, the outage would not happen."
B "If we had tested the edge case, the outage would not have happened."
C "If we test the edge case, the outage would not have happened."
You disagree with a manager, but you want to sound diplomatic. You say:
A "It might be worth waiting until Monday, rather than deploying on Friday."
B "You are wrong; we must not deploy on Friday."
C "You should to wait until Monday."
A teammate raises a serious issue that blocks the release. You say:
A "That is a blocker, so let's escalate it and follow up today."
B "That is a trade-off, so let's just deploy it anyway."
C "That is an edge case, so we can ignore it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give your own midpoint review. Talk about how far your English has come since you started, then use one reported quote from a colleague, one conditional about a past incident, and one diplomatic recommendation for your team. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1If we had caught it sooner, the outage would have been shorter.
2She said the root cause was a bad config.
3It might be worth rolling back, rather than shipping tonight.
4Let me signpost my answer: there are two parts to this.
5I will follow up on that and get back to you.

Complementary Activities

Optional extras to enjoy outside class. Mark each one as done after you watch it.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Ana? -- Introducing Your Work in QA & Tech

Grammar Video
Present Simple and Present Continuous -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the difference between what you do every day (present simple) and what is happening right now (present continuous). Connection to Lesson 1: this is the contrast behind the classic mistake "I am working here for six years" -- once you feel these two tenses, choosing the present perfect for your experience becomes natural.

Tip: pause after each example and say one true sentence about your own work -- one with present simple, one with present continuous.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Business English
Business English B1-B2: Participating in Meetings 1 (Elena Adamova)

Practical language for taking part in a work meeting in English: giving your opinion, agreeing, disagreeing politely, and asking questions. Connection to Lesson 1: exactly the confident, real-time speaking you are building for remote calls and interviews with an international team.

Tip: write down 5 phrases you could use in your next stand-up. Say each one out loud twice.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
How to Stay Calm When You Know You'll Be Stressed (Daniel Levitin, TED)

A neuroscientist explains how to prepare for high-pressure moments so your mind stays clear. Connection to Lesson 1: the nervousness that blocks your speaking in fast conversations is exactly this -- and the ideas here help you stay calm in an interview or a live call.

Tip: listen for the main idea first, without stopping. Then watch again and note one strategy you can try before your next English call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Your Career Story -- Talking About Your Experience

Grammar Video
Present Perfect with "for" and "since" -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact contrast at the heart of Lesson 2: "for" with a period of time (for six years) and "since" with a starting point (since 2018). This is the mistake to avoid when you talk about how long you have worked in QA.

Tip: after each example, pause and say one true sentence about your own career -- one with "for" and one with "since".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Video
Present Perfect with "ever" and "never" -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

The other half of Lesson 2: how to ask about experience ("Have you ever led a team?") and how to say you have no experience of something ("I have never worked abroad"). Exactly the questions a recruiter asks and the honest answers you will give.

Tip: write three "Have you ever...?" questions you could ask an interviewer, and one honest "I have never..." answer about yourself.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Interview Skills
Job Interview in English: "Tell Me About Yourself" (Christina Rebuffet)

A practical lesson on structuring your answer to the most common interview question. Connection to Lesson 2: this is exactly where you tell your career story with the present perfect -- how long you have worked, your expertise, and the milestones you have achieved.

Tip: build your own 60-second answer. Use two present perfect sentences with "for/since" and one with a milestone. Record it and listen back.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — A Day in QA -- Describing Your Routine

Grammar Video
Present Simple and Present Continuous -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact contrast at the heart of Lesson 3: the present simple for your routine and the present continuous for what is happening right now. This is the switch you make every time you describe your workday.

Tip: after each example, pause and say one true sentence about yourself -- one with "every day" and one with "right now".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Lesson
100 TOP Daily Routine Phrases: Advanced English Lesson (Speak English With Vanessa)

A rich set of natural phrases for describing your daily routine in English. Connection to Lesson 3: you will hear dozens of present simple sentences about a typical day, exactly the kind you use to explain your workflow to a teammate.

Tip: choose five phrases that fit your real workday and adapt them to QA -- "First, I check the backlog. Then I prioritize the bugs."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Work Skills
Agile, Explained: Daily Standup Meetings (U.S. GAO)

A clear, professional explanation of the daily stand-up -- the fifteen-minute meeting at the center of your QA day. It shows how each person shares what they finished, what they are working on, and any blocker: the exact updates you give in English.

Tip: prepare your own stand-up update in three sentences -- yesterday, today (right now), and one blocker. Say it out loud before your next meeting.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Reading a Bug Report -- Listening for Detail

Grammar Video
Past simple and past continuous -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact contrast at the heart of Lesson 4: the past simple for a completed event and the past continuous for an action in progress. This is how you describe what you were doing when the bug appeared.

Tip: after each example, pause and say one true sentence about your work with "I was ...-ing when ...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Tech English
Software Testing Tutorial #45 -- How to Write a Good Defect Report (Software Testing Mentor)

A practical QA lesson, in clear English, on the fields of a real bug report: title, steps to reproduce, expected and actual result, severity, and environment -- exactly the words from Lesson 4, in your own field.

Tip: pause on each field and say it out loud with a bug you have found. Notice how often the report uses the past tenses.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Listening Practice
30 Minutes of English Listening Practice | Intermediate B1-B2! (Max & Mia Podcast)

Slow, natural conversation to train the exact skill from Lesson 4: listening for detail. Focus on catching specific facts -- times, numbers, and steps -- the same way you catch the details in a developer's voice message.

Tip: before you play, write two questions you want to answer. Listen once for the gist, then again for the detail.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — The Daily Stand-Up -- Giving Updates

Grammar Video
Present perfect and past simple -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact contrast at the heart of Lesson 5: the past simple for a finished time, and the present perfect for news and results now. This is how you say "I tested it yesterday" and "I have found two bugs so far".

Tip: after each rule, pause and say one true update about your work with "Yesterday, I ..." and "So far, I have ...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Work Context
Agile, Explained: Daily Standup Meetings (U.S. GAO)

A calm, clear explainer of the meeting you practiced tonight: why teams meet every morning, the three questions each person answers, and how blockers become action items. It is your stand-up, in your own field of work.

Tip: after watching, prepare your own three answers -- what you did yesterday, what you are doing today, and any blocker.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Listening Practice
Sound Fluent & Professional: English Phrases for Meetings & Updates (Speak Confident English)

Natural, clear English for the exact skill from Lesson 5: giving a professional update. Listen for the ready-made phrases you can use to report progress, flag a blocker, and follow up -- the language of a confident stand-up.

Tip: write down two phrases you like, then use them out loud in your next stand-up or team message.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Understanding Different Accents -- Indian English

Pronunciation
Why English Native Speakers Sound So Fast | Connected Speech Explained (Pronunciation with Emma)

The reason a new accent or a fast pace feels hard: speakers link and blend their words. Emma shows the patterns behind that speed, so the flow of a call stops sounding like one long word and starts sounding like sentences you can follow.

Tip: play a 30-second part twice. The first time, catch only the main idea; the second time, notice where the words link together.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Skill
Polite requests and questions | Spoken English (Crown Academy of English)

The exact language from this lesson: how to ask for something politely with "Could you...?" and how to form the questions you use to clarify and confirm on a call. Clear, slow, and easy to copy.

Tip: after each example, pause and build your own clarifying question: "Could you repeat...?", "Could you spell...?".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Business Calls
Telephone Conversations: 45 Essential Phrases To Speak On The Phone in English (mmmEnglish)

A ready set of phrases for exactly the moments in a call where you need to check what you heard: asking someone to repeat, to slow down, and to confirm a name or number. Perfect for your remote stand-ups and dev calls.

Tip: pick three phrases you like and say them out loud until they feel automatic, so they are ready on your next real call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Explaining a Test Scenario -- Sequencing

Grammar Video
How to use sequencers in English: FIRST, THEN, NEXT, AFTER THAT, FINALLY (Learn English with Papa Teach Me)

A clear, friendly lesson on the exact linkers from Lesson 7: first, then, next, after that, and finally. This is the language you use to explain any process in order, from a test case to a recipe.

Tip: after the video, describe one work task out loud using all five sequencers, in order.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Work Context
Software Testing Tutorial #32 -- How to Write Test Cases (Software Testing Mentor)

A practical walk-through of the exact skill from tonight's lesson: how a QA analyst writes a test case, step by step, with the precondition, the steps, the input, and the expected outcome. It is your own field, in clear English.

Tip: pause and say each field of a test case out loud in English -- precondition, steps, input, expected outcome.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Language Skill
Imperatives: giving instructions (British Council | ESOL Nexus)

Short and clear practice for the language of instructions -- "first, do this; then, do that". Perfect for walking a teammate through a scenario one step at a time, exactly like the role-play tonight.

Tip: turn one step of your test into an instruction, like "First, open the login page. Then enter the email."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Checkpoint -- Review of Block 1

Grammar Review
Present Perfect and the Past Simple | English Conversation about Experiences (Learn English by Pocket Passport)

The exact contrast from your review meeting: the present perfect for experience up to now ("I have tested many features") versus the past simple for a finished moment ("In week five, I tested one"). A natural conversation, not a dry rule.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about your first weeks of QA work.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Review
How to Use the Past Simple and Past Continuous with WHEN & WHILE + Quiz (English with Em)

A clear review of two Block 1 tenses together: the past simple for the completed action and the past continuous for the action in progress around it. The short quiz at the end lets you check what you remember.

Tip: after the lesson, describe your week-three blocker: "I was testing the checkout when the environment broke."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Review
Learn English Tenses: The Present Continuous (Learn English with Bob the Canadian)

Rounds out your tense review: the present continuous for what is happening right now ("Right now I am working on an edge case"), next to the present simple you use for your daily routine. Calm and easy to follow.

Tip: contrast the two out loud — "Every morning I check the backlog" (routine) versus "Right now I am checking a bug" (now).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Talking About Requirements -- Asking & Confirming

Grammar Video
Indirect Questions -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact structure from tonight: polite, indirect questions like "Could you tell me where the office is?" Notice how the word order changes and the do/does disappears -- the same rule you use to ask about requirements.

Tip: after the video, turn three direct questions about your work into indirect ones out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Work Context
5 Powerful Requirement Gathering Techniques Every Business Analyst Should Know (Bridging the Gap)

The exact skill from tonight's lesson, in your own field: how a professional gathers requirements by asking the right questions, clarifying the scope, and confirming what the end user really needs. It is the English of a real requirements call.

Tip: pause after each technique and say one question you could ask a product owner about a feature you test.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Language Skill
12 Expressions to Ask for Clarification (American Business English -- Tannia Suarez)

Twelve natural phrases to ask for clarification and confirm what you heard on a call, in American English. Perfect for the moment a requirement is ambiguous and you need to check the scope, the constraint, or the acceptance criteria before you test.

Tip: pick three expressions and use them the next time a ticket is unclear at work.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Reporting a Problem -- Being Clear & Precise

Grammar
How to Use the Passive Voice -- English Grammar Lesson (mmmEnglish)

The exact grammar of this class: the passive voice (be + past participle) and when to choose it -- when the action or the result matters more than who did it. Emma is clear and easy to follow at your level.

Tip: after each example, turn one of your own report lines into the passive, like "A patch was deployed."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar
How to Use the Active and Passive Voice -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, focused lesson that puts the active next to the passive, so you can hear when to switch. It is the neutral, professional voice you want for an incident report to stakeholders.

Tip: pause at each pair and say the passive version out loud: "The issue was escalated", "The problem has been resolved."

Watch on YouTube ↗
QA at Work
How to Write a Good Bug Report -- Step-by-Step Guide with Real Examples (ClickUp)

The skill of this class in your own field: writing a report that is clear, precise and concise. Listen for how a strong report leads with the impact, gives a reproducible symptom, and stays short -- exactly what Ana does for her stakeholders.

Tip: after watching, write your own five-line report about one bug -- impact first, and use the passive voice for what was done.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — If This, Then That -- First Conditional

Grammar Video
The First Conditional -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

A short, clear lesson on the exact structure from tonight: "If it rains, I will take my umbrella." Listen for the pattern -- present simple after "if", "will" in the result -- the same one you use to talk about cause and effect in testing.

Tip: after the video, turn three real risks at your work into first conditional sentences out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Language Skill
First Conditional -- Grammar & Verb Tenses (Ellii, formerly ESL Library)

A second, slower walk-through of the first conditional with more examples of cause and effect. It reinforces the two classic mistakes to avoid: no "will" after "if", and don't forget the third-person "-s" in the present simple.

Tip: pause after each example and predict the result before the video says it.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Work Context
Performance Testing Tutorial for Beginners (Simplilearn)

The cause-and-effect ideas from tonight in your own field: what happens to a system as the load grows, where the bottleneck appears, and why a threshold matters. It is the English of a real load and performance test.

Tip: for each idea, say one first conditional sentence, like "If the load doubles, the response time will grow."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — What Would You Do? -- Second Conditional

Grammar
The Second Conditional -- 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

The exact grammar of this class in six short minutes: If + past simple, would + base verb, for imagined situations. It is the clean, neutral British English explanation of the pattern you use to weigh trade-offs and plan for "what if".

Tip: after each example, make your own test-planning sentence, like "If I had more time, I would automate the checkout."

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar
How to Use the 2nd Conditional -- British English Grammar Lesson (Love English)

A slower, example-rich walk through the second conditional, with plenty of natural sentences. Great for hearing the rhythm of "If I had..., I would..." and how native speakers really use it to talk about unreal situations.

Tip: pause and answer the question out loud: "What would you do if the tests failed one hour before the release?"

Watch on YouTube ↗
QA at Work
Risk-Based Testing -- Deciding What to Test When You Cannot Test Everything (TestinGil)

The theme of this class in your own field: if you had all the time in the world you could test everything, but you never do -- so you weigh risk and impact and decide what matters most. Listen for the same decision-making Ana does before her release.

Tip: after watching, describe one real trade-off in the second conditional: "If I tested this by hand, I would be safe but slow."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Used To, Be Used To, Get Used To -- Talking About Change and Adaptation

Grammar Video
Used To, Be Used To, Get Used To (Arnel's Everyday English)

A clear, natural explanation of the exact three structures from tonight, with plenty of real examples. Listen for how a native speaker moves between a past habit ("I used to...") and being or becoming accustomed ("I'm used to..." / "I'm getting used to...").

Tip: after the video, say one sentence of each type about your own work out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Language Skill
Used To, Get Used To, Be Used To in English (Lingportal)

A second, slower walk-through that focuses on the two classic mistakes to avoid: dropping the "-d" in "used to", and using a base verb instead of "-ing" after "be/get used to". Great reinforcement before you speak in the lesson.

Tip: pause after each example and predict the correct form before the video says it.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Mindset Talk
The Power of Belief -- Mindset and Success (Eduardo Briceno, TEDx)

The theme of tonight in context: how a growth mindset helps you embrace change and keep adjusting when things feel unfamiliar. It is a clear, motivating talk about adaptation and resilience, in slow, natural English.

Tip: while you watch, note one habit you used to have and one you are getting used to now.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Advanced Modals -- Would Rather, Had Better, May As Well

Grammar
Would Rather vs Had Better -- the Difference (Englishing)

A short, clear video on two of the three modals from this class: "would rather" for what you prefer, and "had better" for firm advice. It shows the exact difference you use to state a preference and then warn your team about a deadline.

Tip: after each example, build your own tech sentence, like "I would rather use the free tool" and "we had better decide this week".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar
May As Well / Might As Well -- English Grammar Lesson (EnglishLK)

The third modal of the class, explained with everyday examples: "may as well" for when there is no better option, so you might as well do the useful thing. This is the pattern Ana uses to turn dead time into progress.

Tip: pause and finish this sentence out loud: "We are blocked anyway, so we may as well ..."

Watch on YouTube ↗
QA at Work
How to Choose the Right Tool -- 11 Steps for Your Next Test (Vasanth Tech Talk)

The theme of this class in your own field: how a QA engineer actually decides between testing tools -- weighing the upside, the downside and what is viable for the team. Listen for the same reasoning Ana uses before she writes her recommendation.

Tip: after watching, recommend one tool out loud in the modals of the class: "I would rather...", "we had better...", "we may as well...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Hedging Language -- Sounding Measured and Diplomatically Precise

Language Skill
Being Polite -- Soften Your Language (BBC Learning English)

The exact skill from tonight: how native speakers soften opinions, problems and requests so they do not sound blunt. Listen for the modals and cautious phrases that keep a message diplomatic.

Tip: note three softening phrases and try them in your next work message.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Business English
How To Be More Diplomatic & Polite (Derek Callan)

A practical business lesson on hedging in meetings: how to give an opinion, disagree, and deliver bad news without sounding rude. Perfect for your international standups.

Tip: pause after each blunt example and say the diplomatic version yourself before he does.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Conversation Skills
How to Disagree in English Politely (Speak Confident English)

A focused lesson on the hardest hedge of all: disagreeing without contradicting. Learn phrases like "I'm not sure that's quite right" that keep the conversation open, exactly what Ana does in the standup.

Tip: choose one polite disagreement phrase and use it the next time you would normally say "no".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Third Conditional and Mixed Conditionals -- Rewriting Outcomes in Postmortems

Grammar
Learn English Grammar: The 3rd Conditional (engVid)

A clear, structured lesson on the third conditional -- if + past perfect, would have + past participle -- the exact form Ana uses to say what the team did not do and what would not have happened.

Tip: after each example, build your own postmortem sentence, like "if we had tested it in staging, the outage would not have happened".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar
Grammar: Mixing Conditionals (BBC Learning English)

A short masterclass on mixed conditionals: connecting a past cause to a present result, and a present truth to a past result. This is the pattern Ana uses to link the past mistake to the present pain in her retrospective.

Tip: pause and finish this sentence out loud: "If we had documented that near miss, we would not be ...".

Watch on YouTube ↗
QA at Work
Mastering the Blameless Retrospective / Postmortem (Blameless)

The theme of this class in your own field: how engineering teams run a blameless postmortem after an incident -- building a timeline, finding contributing factors, and turning hindsight into preventable actions. Listen for the same reasoning Ana uses in her review.

Tip: after watching, run your own mini postmortem out loud, using one third conditional and one clear, preventable takeaway.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Reported Speech -- Capturing What Was Said in Meetings and Escalations

Grammar Lesson
Learn to Use Reported Speech in English (engVid, Ronnie)

The core grammar from tonight, step by step: how to move from someone's exact words to a report, and how the tense shifts one step back. Clear, slow, and full of examples.

Tip: pause after each direct sentence and say the reported version yourself before she does.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Practice
Can You Use Reported Speech? Grammar Lesson + Examples (mmmEnglish)

A practical lesson focused on reporting statements and questions, including the backshift and the tricky reported questions with "asked if / whether" that you practiced in the debrief.

Tip: note three reported questions and try rebuilding them from the original questions.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Grammar Deep-Dive
Reported Speech: Everything You Need To Know! (Espresso English)

A complete review of reported speech: statements, questions, commands, and how time and place words change. Perfect for locking in the backshift before you report a real call at work.

Tip: after watching, report one thing a colleague told you today, keeping one detail verbatim.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Speaking Without a Script -- Holding the Floor and Staying Fluent

Speaking
"That's a Good Question..." -- How to Buy Time While Speaking (Go Natural English)

A short, practical lesson on the exact move Ana uses on her call: stalling with a natural phrase to buy a second before you answer, instead of freezing or rambling. Perfect for the moment a question goes off-script.

Tip: pick two stalling phrases from the video and say them out loud until they feel automatic, like "that's a great question, let me think for a moment".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Communication
Think Fast, Talk Smart: Communication Techniques (Stanford GSB)

A widely shared talk by Matt Abrahams on speaking spontaneously and staying calm when you have to answer without preparation -- the whole skill of this class. He explains how to handle anxiety, signpost your ideas, and improvise a structured answer on the spot.

Tip: notice his advice on using simple structures (like "what, so what, now what") to signpost. Try one on your next surprise question.

Watch on YouTube ↗
TED Talk
How to Speak So That People Want to Listen (Julian Treasure, TED)

A classic TED talk on holding the floor and speaking with control: pace, silence, and clarity. It shows why pausing on purpose -- instead of filling every gap with "um" -- makes you sound more fluent and confident, exactly what Ana does when she stalls to buy time.

Tip: after watching, record one minute of yourself explaining a QA result, using a deliberate pause instead of a filler word.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Midpoint Milestone -- Progress Review and Fluency Benchmark

Grammar Review
Compare ALL English Conditional Sentences (mmmEnglish)

A single clear lesson comparing the zero, first, second and third conditionals side by side, with examples. Perfect for reviewing the "if we had tested it, the outage would not have happened" structure from tonight.

Tip: pause after each type and build your own example from a real incident at work.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Speaking Skills
How to Disagree in English Politely (Speak Confident English)

Practical phrases for pushing back without sounding blunt: the diplomatic modals you reviewed tonight, like "it might be worth..." and "if I were you...". Exactly what you need for the second half.

Tip: note three softening phrases and use one the next time you disagree on a call.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Motivation Talk
How to Learn Any Language in Six Months (TEDx, Chris Lonsdale)

A short, inspiring talk on how adults actually become fluent, and why the halfway point is where confidence takes over from vocabulary. A great reminder as you start the second half of your program.

Tip: pick one principle from the talk and apply it to your English this week.

Watch on YouTube ↗
